emails.subject: JPM TECH SKETCH: SE/GRAB/GOTO Q2 Previews, MSFT Another -2.5% RIF Coming, META Tried Buying Kalshi - Sorta
emails.sender: Mark Schilsky - Specialist Sales - US TMT
emails.body:
Slow News Day, ASEAN Internet Q226 Previews, META Serious About PMs (re: DKNG), Wayve Making AV Waves (re: UBER) 


JPM TECH SKETCH: SE/GRAB/GOTO Q2 Previews,MSFT Another -2.5% RIF Coming, META Tried Buying Kalshi - Sorta 


Mark Schilsky | mark.schilsky@jpmorgan.com | +1 (212) 622-2668 
вҖӢ 


SCHILSKYвҖҷS SNAPSHOT 


Welcome to Q3. 

- SE/GRAB/GOTO Q226 Earnings Previews: From RanjanвҖҷs SE/GRAB/GOTO Q226 Earnings Previews <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=5g2QUZy&sig=Q6f6eYUImAvPoOXIVGlMLFJj5zX&v=2&isig=9vPVrgFn0jLJi6potAh4xYkbA4G&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-sit9hb3/GPS-5352365-0/ab72eece-5a18-41ee-b960-48089391f667> : вҖңASEAN internet stocks have been weak this year (SE: -27%, GRAB: -25%, GOTO:-22%, NASDAQ: +11%) due to worries about competition, macro impact, and the broader de-rating of internet stocksвҖҰWhile we anticipate continued strong growth at Shopee, we believe margins are unlikely to inflect in 2Q26E.вҖқ 

- MSFT Another -2.5% RIF is Coming: According to Business Insider <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=GIYOWOU&sig=47DZgwYwkV8ysT2rIGfK5T8jDRq&v=2&url=https://www.businessinsider.com/microsoft-job-cuts-layoffs-sales-consulting-2026-6> . Sounds like next week. Investors wonвҖҷt really care; all they care aboutu is top-line. Accelerate or die*. 

- META Tried Buying Kalshi - Sorta (re: DKNG/FLUT): According to NPR <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=Gy19oZm&sig=D3JWxYyMBFvbXvIcColqci4qOfs&v=2&url=https://www.npr.org/2026/06/30/nx-s1-5875468/meta-kalshi-prediction-market-acquisition-talks?secureweb%3Dbplus64> . 

- META Loses Bid to Dismiss US State Lawsuits: Just continue to keep an eye on these types of headlines. Reuters <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=rodphtB&sig=GnDSOPDQ73oaO5yzzhpPZWj0y78&v=2&url=https://www.reuters.com/legal/government/meta-loses-bid-dismiss-us-states-claims-that-facebook-instagram-addict-children-2026-06-30/>  

- JPM Research: SE/GRAB/GOTO Q226 Earnings Previews <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=5g2QUZy&sig=Q6f6eYUImAvPoOXIVGlMLFJj5zX&v=2&isig=9vPVrgFn0jLJi6potAh4xYkbA4G&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-sit9hb3/GPS-5352365-0/ab72eece-5a18-41ee-b960-48089391f667>  

- JPM Events:  Media Q226 Earnings Preview Webinar (DIS, LYV, TKO, FOXA, etc.) Tuesday July 28th at 10AM - with David Karnovsky & Mark Schilsky  REGISTER HERE <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=ZFWBaAZ&sig=XQy0yOj4iGfN1mXAXpvsWhoAui8&v=2&url=https://jpmchase.zoom.us/webinar/register/WN_P8j3yfCURCqs-iS-BY7w0Q%23/registration>  

AVGO CEO/CFO Virtual Group NDR Tuesday July 7th Request a Spot Here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=wqeh6vO&sig=7Kv4lrn4Vz4iiigu4h0kkuOZDDS&v=2&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/event_booking/loadEvent?eventToken%3D137025_d692e505-aec2-41bb-ab39-71c89bb77a08>  



NEWS вҖ“ DESK COLOR вҖ“ RESEARCH HIGHLIGHTS 


SE/GRAB/GOTO Q226 Earnings Previews: 


From RanjanвҖҷs SE/GRAB/GOTO Q226 Earnings Previews <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=5g2QUZy&sig=Q6f6eYUImAvPoOXIVGlMLFJj5zX&v=2&isig=9vPVrgFn0jLJi6potAh4xYkbA4G&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-sit9hb3/GPS-5352365-0/ab72eece-5a18-41ee-b960-48089391f667> : вҖңASEAN internet stocks have been weak this year (SE: -27%, GRAB: -25%, GOTO:-22%, NASDAQ: +11%) due to worries about competition, macro impact, and the broader de-rating of internet stocks. In our view, GRABвҖҷs 2Q26 could dispel many of the concerns with continued strong growth in on-demand GMV, market-share gains and stable margins. Any addition to the existing buyback program is likely to be positively received, given the stock is at a 2028E EV/EBITDA multiple of 8x. While we anticipate continued strong growth at Shopee, we believe margins are unlikely to inflect in 2Q26E. Given the StreetвҖҷs focus on ShopeeвҖҷs margins, we believe GRAB could perform better than SE with 2Q26 results. Taking a long-term view, we remain OW on both SE and GRAB as we believe the Street is underestimating the earnings potential of both platforms.вҖқ 

*	вҖңGRAB вҖ“ Market share gains likely to have continued into 2Q26. We anticipate mobility and delivery GMV growth of 18% and 20%, respectively. In our view, GRAB is outpacing industry growth and gaining market share, driven by its product-led initiatives and platform advantage. We believe that on-demand industry shows strong network effects which are benefitting GRAB. We forecast 2Q26E adj EBITDA of $160m (+47% yoy, +4% qoq). In our view, GRAB should comfortably meet its 2026 adj EBITDA guidance of $700-720m (JPMe: $715m) with upside risk arising from the potential easing of macro headwinds in the region. Our model does not yet incorporate the acquisition of Foodpanda Taiwan and consolidation of Superbank.вҖқ 
*	вҖңSE вҖ“ Shopee likely to deliver strong growth but margins yet to inflect. We forecast 26% yoy GMV growth for Shopee in 2Q26. However, with continued investments (e.g. VIP Program), we believe any margin improvement (2Q26E JPMe: 0.61% of GMV) is likely to be marginal despite the increases in take-rates. We anticipate continued growth in the fintech business with adj. EBITDA growing to $287m (+12% yoy, +4% qoq). Our growth expectations are driven by the growing credit portfolio, product suite and customers across SEвҖҷs geographies. We anticipate gaming to see a sharp decline in gross bookings by 21% qoq as 1Q26 benefited from content collaboration.вҖқ 
*	вҖңGOTO вҖ“ Top-line growth is likely to be modest. We forecast ODS GTV growth to remain modest at 3% qoq/yoy. Adj EBITDA for ODS could also see a sequential decline in 2Q/3Q26E vs 1Q26 due to changes in Indonesia's ride-hailing policies, including a reduction in 2W commission. For fintech, payment-led GTV growth should continue, but we expect a more conservative approach to loan disbursement given the tougher macro environment. We expect credit costs to remain at an elevated level of >35%, but the business should remain profitable given its >50% loan yields. For fintech, we forecast 2Q26E adj EBITDA of IDR360bn (flat qoq, >300% yoy). For the Group, we forecast 2Q26E adj EBITDA of IDR846bn (+98% yoy, -7% qoq).вҖқ 

________________________________


Wayve Courts Automakers for AVs (re: UBER, LYFT, TSLA, GOOGL): 


From Reuters <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=An-KPnZ&sig=UAuprgbGGvgwvkv49aaKsQj7K5F&v=2&url=https://www.reuters.com/technology/wayve-courts-automakers-with-ai-driving-system-that-learns-like-humans-2026-07-01/> : вҖңThe London-based company has pulled in $2.8 billion from a roster of investors and strategic partners that includes big names across the technology and automotive sectors, from Nvidia to Mercedes-Benz and Nissan. In June, Wayve said it will deploy its system in robotaxis from Jeep maker Stellantis, to go on Uber's ride-hailing network. Wayve uses an artificial-intelligence technology called end-to-end machine learning to navigate roadways, which is supposed to instantly translate sensor-generated data into driving decisions, much like a human driver. That differs from a more traditional approach, which melds AI with software coding and high-definition maps to create preset rules for how the car should respond in different scenarios, including unforeseen events. WayveвҖҷs approach is similar to another big autonomous-driving player вҖ” Tesla , which moved to an end-to-end model a few years ago. Unlike TeslaвҖҷs approach, though, which uses cameras as its only onboard sensor set, WayveвҖҷs system is designed to work with a wide array of sensors and AI chips.вҖқ 

________________________________


MSFT Another -2.5% RIF is Coming: 


According to Business Insider <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=GIYOWOU&sig=47DZgwYwkV8ysT2rIGfK5T8jDRq&v=2&url=https://www.businessinsider.com/microsoft-job-cuts-layoffs-sales-consulting-2026-6> : вҖңMicrosoft is planning to announce job cuts soon as the tech giant continues efforts to control costs, according to people familiar with the situation. The cuts are expected to impact thousands of roles, including sales and consulting, in addition to jobs at the Xbox gaming division, the people said. This round is expected to be smaller than similar layoffs last year. This time, the cuts are projected to be less than 2.5% of the company's 220,000-person workforce, the people addedвҖҰThe company is planning to announce the layoffs next week, although the exact timing could change.вҖқ 

 
________________________________


META Tried Buying Kalshi - Sorta (re: DKNG/FLUT): 


According to NPR <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=Gy19oZm&sig=D3JWxYyMBFvbXvIcColqci4qOfs&v=2&url=https://www.npr.org/2026/06/30/nx-s1-5875468/meta-kalshi-prediction-market-acquisition-talks?secureweb%3Dbplus64> : вҖңBefore Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg directed employees to build a standalone prediction market app, he proposed buying Kalshi, the leading company in the prediction market sector, according to three people with knowledge of the discussions who were not authorized to speak publicly. Zuckerberg met with Kalshi CEO Tarek Mansour about a possible takeover last year as Kalshi's popularity surged, but the negotiations never advanced, according to one of the people who had direct knowledge of the meeting. There are competing narratives about why the talks broke down, with some saying Mansour would not move forward with a sale and others indicating Meta considered the legal and ethical questions surrounding Kalshi too messy.вҖқ 

________________________________


META Loses Bid to Dismiss US State Lawsuits: 


Just continue to keep an eye on these types of headlines.

From Reuters <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=rodphtB&sig=GnDSOPDQ73oaO5yzzhpPZWj0y78&v=2&url=https://www.reuters.com/legal/government/meta-loses-bid-dismiss-us-states-claims-that-facebook-instagram-addict-children-2026-06-30/> : вҖңA вҖӢfederal judge rejected Meta Platforms' bid to dismiss a lawsuit by 29 U.S. state attorneys general вҖҢaccusing it of designing Facebook and Instagram to addict children and knowingly concealing the harm from the public. In a decision late Monday, U.S. District Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers in Oakland, California, denied Meta's motion to dismiss claims based on deception, unfair practices and violations вҖӢof the federal Children's Online Privacy Protection Act. The judge also said Meta did not comply with вҖӢthat law's notice and parental consent requirements, and granted summary judgment to the states on вҒ that issue. Meta said in a statement: вҖңWe strongly disagree with these allegations and are confident the evidence will show вҖӢour longstanding commitment to supporting young people."вҖқ 

________________________________


Why Internet Stocks CanвҖҷt Work (Redux): 


[IвҖҷm merely reprinting this piece from yesterday because it really is that slow of a news day.] 

Internet investor sentiment is currently as low as IвҖҷve seen it in over the past four years. Consistently, I hear from investors that вҖҳXYZ stock canвҖҷt work becauseвҖҰвҖҷ without any related discussion of what could make the stocks work. As a result, IвҖҷve decided to compile the most commonly cited reasons IвҖҷve heard as to why Internet stocks canвҖҷt work. In theory, if any of these overhangs start to lift, these stocks may begin to work. In theoryвҖҰ 

*	META: ROIC on ZuckerbergвҖҷs Superintelligence Lab spend is currently highly negative. Without any new products and/or model releases, investors do not yet know if METAвҖҷs AI efforts will lead to a product cycle that looks like Reels вҖ“ or one that looks like the Metaverse. Plus, there remains a significant equity issuance overhang post-GOOGL. 
*	AMZN: Take your pick of possible overhangs: a potentially weakening US consumer, 2026/2027 Capex estimates going much higher (driven materially by higher component pricing), increasing competition amongst hyperscalers and neoclouds, a desire to compete in space via Leo, a potential equity issuance, etc. AMZN remains the most frustrating Internet stock for the most investors. 
*	GOOGL: Post-equity issuance, investors have ratcheted expectations for FY27 Capex estimates meaningfully higher. IвҖҷve heard anywhere from $300B to $400B; the Street remains way too low at ~$245B. Until investors have clarity on what this figure will be, the stock canвҖҷt work without commensurate Revenue acceleration beyond what we already saw in Q1. (Maybe on the Q2 print?) 
*	RDDT: The only two ways that RDDT can dispel the AI Bear Case that products like GOOGL AI Overviews will obviate the need for an increasing number of internet users to peruse RDDT forums are: 1) Materially inflect US DAUs for more than one quarter. [Yes, RDDTвҖҷs stock would pop if they did this on the Q226 print, but this has to be a sustainable trend above +10% Y/Y to get investors to really pay attention.] & 2) Sign a new GOOGL AI content licensing deal <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=B4Wu3s2&sig=WYb2k6pYfUDU7hYzNOugZycdgOx&v=2&url=https://www.reuters.com/technology/reddit-ai-content-licensing-deal-with-google-sources-say-2024-02-22/> . Not for materially more money, although that would be nice, but for guaranteed traffic. The persistent RDDT overhang is that GOOGL is just one algo change away from crushing referral traffic to RDDT; a new AI deal with some type of traffic guarantees would be welcomed by investors вҖ“ perhaps in early 2027. 
*	APP: While there remain two most-often-cited reasons why APP canвҖҷt work, in my opinion, only one can ever truly go away. The first issue is META. While there have been conflicting boutique research reports about the possibility of META entering the non-IDFA mobile games space since January, the unfortunate reality for APP shareholders is that this boogeyman will never fully go away. As such, only the second reason can truly go away in the next few quarters: eComm. APPвҖҷs stock saw a massive expansion in its multiple in 2025 as investors increasingly got excited about the eComm opportunity, but near-term eComm enthusiasm has been tempered by management вҖ“ both on earnings calls and in meetings. The AppLovin ad platform just opened up to self-serve a week ago and there is a good chance that eComm can inflect in Q4, but I wouldnвҖҷt necessarily assume in Q3. In other words, APP canвҖҷt work until eComm demonstrably inflects. 
*	NFLX: Netflix canвҖҷt work because US engagement growth in the low single-digits remains anemic. Engagement is a harbinger of subscriber growth (which is negative in the US if you believe the alt data for Q2) and NFLX is either going to have to increase cash content spend in order to drive engagement/subscriber growth (on a tough 2H comp) or engage in some type of scaled M&A. Either way, the persistent investor view I hear is that NFLX is slowly morphing into a cable TV network (low subscriber growth, increasingly reliant on advertising) and thus the multiple should head lower. 
*	SPOT: Despite providing investors with nearly everything they wanted, Spotify hasnвҖҷt worked since the May Investor Day because feedback from the labels has been that the AI Product Tier isnвҖҷt yet ready for primetime. It will take all three major labels to sign on, not to mention a majority of artists, in order for the product to launch with any type of product-market fit. Initially, the Bullish view was that this could occur by year end, but increasingly this feels like a 2027 story at best. Until investors obtain clarity on where the new AI products are going, the stock canвҖҷt work. 
*	CVNA: Carvana has been a frustrating stock for Internet investors this year despite the company demonstrating consistent ~40% unit growth. The stock вҖҳcanвҖҷt workвҖҷ because itвҖҷs crowded and thereвҖҷs always a looming US consumer-led slowdown just around the cornerвҖҰ 
*	UBER: The AV overhang, as exemplified by GOOGLвҖҷs Waymo, means that UBER canвҖҷt work. Every Internet investor by now has ridden in a Waymo and sees how well the technology works in dense urban environments like SF. WaymoвҖҷs only real limitation at this point is the companyвҖҷs willingness to expand its fleet вҖ“ but itвҖҷs coming. In particular, 50K Hyundais <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=1vcxvsW&sig=LK83uQPv4oBqECUevMx5zZa8oQs&v=2&url=https://electrek.co/2026/02/11/hyundai-supply-waymo-50000-ioniq-5-robotaxis/>  are coming over the next 2.5 years and that will potentially blunt UBERвҖҷs US Mobility growth. As such, UBER вҖҳcanвҖҷt workвҖҷ until one of their AV partners (Nuro, Wayve, Moove, Zoox, VW-MBLY, etc.) pulls US-based safety drivers from their cars at a scale currently beyond TSLA. Check out DougвҖҷs latest: UBER/GOOGL AV Roadmap Update <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518269&ad=4540325718&h=ABiuUR9&sig=RyEUC1AP1xDbMWYxkLl58ScXFSQ&v=2&isig=Q0VLpV5ryvX72szbG3zI3fQCcPM&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/5v1jq7f6/GPS-5315883-0/ab72eece-5a18-41ee-b960-48089391f667> . 
*	DASH: DoorDash canвҖҷt work because the stock requires upward Adj. EBITDA revisions to justify its premium multiple вҖ“ and you wonвҖҷt get an earnings revision cycle until DASH has successfully integrated ROO.LN/Seven Rooms and Tony tells us on an earnings call that the reinvestment cycle is over. Plus, US Orders growth decelerated -400 bps Q/Q last quarter, making investors question whether or not this was a blip or the beginning of a volume-led slowdown. 
*	BKNG: Booking canвҖҷt work because their 2H Room Nights growth guidance implies a modest вҖҳhockey stickвҖҷ in the anticipated growth rate. Plus, the guide assumed the Iran War would end by tomorrow and that unfortunately has not yet happened. Plus, BKNG (and EXPE) are squarely in the crosshairs of the вҖҳAgentic AI Internet Bear CaseвҖҷ in which consumer agents will one day book travel on our behalf directly from hotel websites. 

[You may have noticed that I left certain stocks off this list like EBAY, ABNB, etc. and thatвҖҷs because these stocks have actually been working. ThatвҖҷs not to say that they wonвҖҷt be added to this list one day thoughвҖҰ]

emails.subject: Jefferies Tech: Morning News, July Catalyst Calendar, Brent's Cloud Survey, Stats + Charts, AVGO Note Feedback, Calls to Know
emails.body:
1. Movers + Macro

2. Pre-Market News

3. AVGO Feedback

4. Capex Conversations this Q

5. 1H Stats + Charts

6. Cloud Survey

7. Calls + Events to Know

 

2 attachments: A July catalyst calendar outside of earnings | 1H of вҖҷ26 performance snapshot.

 

- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

Movers + Macro.

 

Equity futures lower this AM after a relatively quiet night / morning of market moving news, with the biggest headline being AnthropicвҖҷs Fable 5 being redeployed.

 

Asia memory names all lower overnight, European telcos trying to rebound after the recent weakness, while SAP and ASML are both up small.

 

With the 1H of вҖҷ26 in the books and the Nasdaq up ~20% and the S&P 500 up ~10%, below were the key contributors to the performance YTD, see further below for more stats / charts.

 

Most glaring to us in the below is that virtually 100% of the returns in the Nasdaq during the 1H of вҖҳ26 have been driven by 10 stocks.

And in the pre-market right now, the most glaring dynamic is the leaders / laggards rotation that weвҖҷre currently seeing as we flip the calendar.

 

Below looks at the pre-market performance for an equal weighted basket of the top 25 and bottom 25 performers during the 1H of вҖҷ26.

- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

Pre-Market News.

 

Anthropic: Last night Anthropic noted <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=D5Lgp6C&sig=Rb2z1TgSB8mKzDd7SJwZzS0l8qb&v=2&url=https://x.com/AnthropicAI/status/2072163884430229756>  that their redeploying Fable 5 вҖңwith a new set of classifiers to target and block more cybersecurity tasks. In the near term, some routine tasks like coding and debugging will fall back to Opus 4.8.вҖқ

 

Autos: The FT noted <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=wO3GRZO&sig=QZ34kjPLF1xaI1OrCh1zB9y97Ly&v=2&url=https://www.ft.com/content/dc7b4dfb-b279-4740-ae71-862a9e267aa9?syn-25a6b1a6%3D1>  last night that Chinese carmakers are rushing to cut their dependence on foreign chips in an echo of how their dominance of battery technology gave them an upper hand in making electric vehicles.

 

DT Board Seats: Announced this AM that they are giving 2 seats to Starboard, this follows their letter <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=ru0nh8x&sig=UkTF8gTfg2dQqfaKTq6t1nHEv8d&v=2&url=https://www.starboardvalue.com/wp-content/uploads/Starboard_Value_LP_Letter_to_DT_Management_and_Board_04.28.2026.pdf>  to the company back in April of this year. DT also announced itвҖҷs holding an Investor Day following FQ2 earnings to outline its path to the вҖңRule of 50вҖқ in FYвҖҷ29 and reiterated its intention to continue returning significant capital to shareholders under its $1B share repurchase authorization.

 

Memory Pricing: Trendforce notes <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=SM0Z5LD&sig=YwYJ6a4tmYfZrigUBzvCUBa3df0&v=2&url=https://www.trendforce.com/news/2026/07/01/insights-memory-spot-price-update-dram-spot-prices-see-gains-in-low-density-ddr4-and-ddr3-amid-sideways-market/>  that DRAM spot prices this week have seen upward momentum in LDDDR4 and DDR3 segments, while the broader market continues to trend sideways. In NAND Flash, suppliers are adjusting quotations to optimize inventory levels, but buyers remain reluctant to accept current pricing, with 512Gb TLC wafer spot prices continuing to decline.

 

   

 

MSFT Job Cuts: Last night Business Insider noted <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=GIYOWOU&sig=LdTe5mUCl6m6gJOF2oSOdcuKLZo&v=2&url=https://www.businessinsider.com/microsoft-job-cuts-layoffs-sales-consulting-2026-6>  that MSFT is planning to announce job cuts soon as they continue efforts to control costs. BI notes that вҖңThe cuts are expected to impact thousands of roles, including sales and consulting, in addition to jobs at the Xbox gaming division.вҖқ

 

TeraFab: Wccftech noted <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=K7n_Y0V&sig=6eTRbCCSqSpC6VZsyCASQguXWcw&v=2&url=https://wccftech.com/musks-terafab-scores-18-year-intel-veteran-as-foundry-manager-for-55-billion-chip-manufacturing-project/>  last night that TerafabвҖҷs 1st major hire is Gary Jiang, an 18-year INTC veteran, where in his latest role he was a factory manager at INTCвҖҷs Arizona facility.

 

- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

AVGO Feedback.

 

Blayne had a good note out yesterday revisiting the AVGO bull case. 

 

His key takes were that вҖҰ AVGO has an improved CYвҖҷ28 visibility, CYвҖҷ27 expectations have been reset after last QвҖҷs high expectations (i.e. chips vs racks), TPU roadmap remains on track and head of expectations, overblown MTK concerns, and a broadening out ASIC customer base.

 

A few observations from our conversations yesterday:

*	Conversations largely skewed negative, вҖңwhatвҖҷs the catalyst?вҖқ, which to us, continues to speak to the short lean / funding short dynamic of large cap compute names.
*	Additionally, folks note that share loss stories in Semis typically are uphill battles.
*	Bulls focused on AVGO's multiple getting more attractive given the recent reset in expectations. Bulls don't think MTK will be able to take as much share as some expect.
*	AVGO's non-ASIC AI portfolio (networking and optical) remains undervalued relative to peers like MRVL, which is a part of the story that most investors seem to overlook, V9i not cancelled or changed.
*	Bears thinking MTK supply chain commentary is not overblown, if they do $30 in '28 most folks think stock doesn't do much and need more than that, and the COT fears down the line. Additional focus on HBM inflation next year.
*	A common thread amongst generalists LOs has been the general confusion around conflicting supply chain data points, and some scar tissue coming out of the last Q.

 

And hereвҖҷs the chart of AVGO vs. SOXX over the last ~3 years  вҖҰ 

 



 

вҖҰ and a very similar chart to NVDA vs. the SOXX too.

 



 

- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

Capex Conversations this Q. 

 

Last Q we got capex upticks from META and GOOGL, while AMZN left their FY capex unchanged. One of the bigger questions we have into earnings for the big Internet companies, is what do they do with the вҖҷ26 capex guides on the Q2 prints.

 

While most of our conversations point to a continued upward bias in capex for вҖҷ27 and вҖҷ28 street #вҖҷs, tactically, the capex conversation is a bit different.

 

Most folks expect a capex increase from AMZN after leaving it unchanged on the last print, conversations point to a revision to > $215B. 

 

GOOGL and META are more split in terms of if folks expect them to touch the guides, with the crowd more in the camp that they leave them unchanged. However, in the same vein, folks note that if GOOGL + META do uptick this Q again, they likely use the excuse of memory inflation. 

 

Recall the capex adjustments from last Q:

Cloud Survey. 

 

BrentвҖҷs out with his survey <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=OHDg1z-&sig=AfHlcEBkIpcFZDPnJTV94BpxSgw&v=2&url=https://javatar.bluematrix.com/links2/secure/html/ab0872cb-fb12-47f1-a2b4-c5bdbc87e487>  of 40 US CIOs on their cloud spending. 

 

Survey results show a stronger '26 with Cloud budgets modestly accelerating to 10.1% in '26 vs. 9.6% in '25 as companies prep for AI, MSFT ahead on spend intent w/ 68% planning to grow spend over next 24 months vs. 45% AMZN and 25% GOOG, migration speeding up as 85% of respondents see >half of workloads in Cloud by '27 vs. 75% today. 

 

Cloud spend growth is accelerating to 10.1% in вҖҳ26 from 9.6% in вҖҷ25:

 



 

Rank the top 3 metrics in terms of importance when selecting a public Cloud vendor:

Video Games.

 

Heaney and team recap <https://jefferies.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13518219&ad=4540295793&h=DuSVqEB&sig=UOsdS6G5enQXr5PxmGmsJszPrrg&v=2&url=https://javatar.bluematrix.com/links2/secure/html/c9dfbf52-5fa4-4b77-910c-d1b682128133>  some of the video games news over the last few weeks noting Fortnite UGC developer payouts have crossed $1B life-to-date (vs. $1.5B from RBLX in just 2025), but on a similar annual pace to last 2 years and remains mainly engagement-based vs in-game UGC transactions. 

 

GOOGLвҖҷs new app fees are live in the US, UK, and EU with potential 5%-10% savings, but primarily prices out 3P web transactions. US Judge hearing on July 16 potentially ends Epic v Google.

 

They also flag gaming hardware prices continue to increase with no end in sight. Both Xbox and PlayStation prices have already increased this generation as a result of rising HW component prices, but last week Xbox increased its prices again $100-$150. The cheapest Xbox Series S is now $500 (was $300 at launch in 2020), with the cheapest PS5 now $600 (vs $400 in 2020).

Inside Apple's Chipflation Dilemma <https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1582015&post_id=204383795&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=50datu&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjozMDI5NTE0NDIsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwNDM4Mzc5NSwiaWF0IjoxNzgyOTA1Nzg1LCJleHAiOjE3ODU0OTc3ODUsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTgyMDE1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.Gb8pFZQzcESKDglaKXU81x4tU7hWYQOG2TRMz37B6Fo> 


[Exclusive] The iPhone maker is facing unprecedented cost expansion, with memory jumping to become the single-largest cost item.

Tim Culpan <https://substack.com/@tculpan> 
Jul 1
 <https://substack.com/@tculpan> 	
 	
 <https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1582015&post_id=204383795&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjozMDI5NTE0NDIsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwNDM4Mzc5NSwicmVhY3Rpb24iOiLinaQiLCJpYXQiOjE3ODI5MDU3ODUsImV4cCI6MTc4NTQ5Nzc4NSwiaXNzIjoicHViLTE1ODIwMTUiLCJzdWIiOiJyZWFjdGlvbiJ9.PRizDcp3qwoTDnia6-wqpHulvdr_d2v9_0Q1wz7R3aM&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=50datu> 	
	 <https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1582015&post_id=204383795&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&isFreemail=true&comments=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjozMDI5NTE0NDIsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwNDM4Mzc5NSwiaWF0IjoxNzgyOTA1Nzg1LCJleHAiOjE3ODU0OTc3ODUsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTgyMDE1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.Gb8pFZQzcESKDglaKXU81x4tU7hWYQOG2TRMz37B6Fo&r=50datu&utm_campaign=email-half-magic-comments&action=post-comment&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email> 	
	 <https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1582015&post_id=204383795&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=50datu&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjozMDI5NTE0NDIsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwNDM4Mzc5NSwiaWF0IjoxNzgyOTA1Nzg1LCJleHAiOjE3ODU0OTc3ODUsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTgyMDE1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.Gb8pFZQzcESKDglaKXU81x4tU7hWYQOG2TRMz37B6Fo> 	
	 <https://substack..pGQ0IPBcMd4usqYiGtEsRr0qXoFFniBReWhm_kTeNaU?&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email> 	
<https://open.substack.com/pub/timculpan/p/inside-apples-chipflation-dilemma?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app> 
READ IN APP <https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ET-_!,w_36,c_scale,f_png,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack.com%2Ficon%2FLucideArrowUpRight%3Fv%3D4%26height%3D36%26fill%3Dnone%26stroke%3D%2523808080%26strokeWidth%3D2> 
 	

Good Evening from Taipei,

AppleвҖҷs decision to raise prices across the board last week took a lot of people by surprise. Customers rued their decision to hold off buying a new MacBook Neo or MacBook Pro after seeing costs jump. 

Many also saw it as another example of Apple flexing its pricing power, convinced that consumers will still buy no matter how much Cupertino charges.

ItвҖҷs the exact opposite.

It was an admission of defeat. Proof that decades of strict supply-chain discipline and buying power no longer hold up. 

Apple had tried for months to save money, my sources tell me. But skyrocketing DRAM costs have driven the price of memory from $30 per phone to over $130 apiece, bringing it above 30% of the cost of building an iPhone, according to my discussions with sources.

In reality, AppleвҖҷs decision to add <https://substack.com/redirect/53226248-3417-4056-88be-bfa64bf16541?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  $100 to a MacBook Neo, $300 to a MacBook Pro, and $500 to a Mac Studio shows AppleвҖҷs waning position in the global supply chain for technology hardware.

	 <https://substack.com/redirect/283c6322-ded9-463c-a43d-c93310cebefd?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 		

The hikes also reveal the companyвҖҷs AchillesвҖҷ heel, where Tim Cook and his team have minimal bargaining power.

Regular readers of Culpium have watched me map out this change over the past six months. 

________________________________

New to Culpium? Subscribe for free. Already reading? Pledge your support. Thanks

Pledge your support <https://substack..NFmy0qKhrOkP44MdRsdDjX-OlVLuxf1RVY8Mr8PiAec?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=204383795> 
________________________________


In January I wrote that Apple is Fighting for TSMC Capacity <https://substack.com/redirect/0b4c4c1a-f5d3-4265-b364-fda95d295e85?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  as Nvidia Takes Center Stage.

	вҖңThe iPhone makerвҖҷs chip designs are no longer guaranteed a place among TSMCвҖҷs almost two dozen fabs. What (CEO CC) Wei probably didnвҖҷt tell Cook is that Apple may no longer be his largest client.вҖқ вҖ” Culpium, 15 January 2026.

Then, in February, I shared that TSMCвҖҷs N2 Node is <https://substack.com/redirect/b45ccdae-fcd6-4eb3-8df5-17d07daed3d2?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> Almost Booked Out for the Next Two Years. The ongoing struggle for TSMC capacity вҖңcame to light during the Covid pandemic when car makers were caught short. The current situation, however, is more acute than ever.вҖқ


Happy Conundrum


By happenstance, Apple soon found itself caught in an interesting conundrum. In April I wrote that it was running out of the binned chips <https://substack.com/redirect/58e112ed-085d-460e-9118-e84e677671c4?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  used in the MacBook Neo and was considering whether to order more. вҖңIf they do proceed with a fresh order вҖҰ then it will most likely need to raise prices of the MacBook Neo.вҖқ A big part of that higher cost, I explained at the time, was the likely need to pay more for its A18 Pro processors. 

Apple did end up ordering that fresh batch, <https://substack.com/redirect/1fabae5e-4107-4c47-ab7a-97d3f76d5780?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  and in May I explained that pricier logic chips werenвҖҷt the only challenge in keeping a lid on costs. вҖңDRAM prices have escalated since the initial production run, driving the NeoвҖҷs bill of materials much higher.вҖқ

And if all of that wasnвҖҷt enough of a hint, there was my very blunt assessment that Memory Makers Are Strangling the Rest of the Tech Industry. <https://substack.com/redirect/17686973-143b-42c6-92ec-edcd530e484e?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  вҖңSK Hynix didnвҖҷt sell more DRAM, it just charged more for it,вҖқ I wrote in April. 

 <https://substack.com/redirect/17686973-143b-42c6-92ec-edcd530e484e?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 
	
Memory Makers Are Strangling the Rest of the Tech Industry <https://substack.com/redirect/17686973-143b-42c6-92ec-edcd530e484e?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 

Tim Culpan <https://substack.com/profile/264465-tim-culpan> 
	В·
	Apr 30
<https://substack.com/redirect/17686973-143b-42c6-92ec-edcd530e484e?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 
Read full story	

So in that context we can see that AppleвҖҷs price hike last week was not sudden, it was not rash, and it wasnвҖҷt Tim Cook displaying some fit of pique. 

It was calculated, planned, and executed over a period of months. Apple could see it coming as far back as six months ago, my sources tell me, and has been preparing the groundwork ever since.

And the messaging was carefully stage managed. 

First was the timing. Cook, as CEO, or his CFO Kevan Parekh could have signaled the price rise back in its April 30 earnings call, or they could have done so in the next event around a month from now. But they chose the end of June, when the message wouldnвҖҷt get lost.

Then they chose the medium. In this case, an interview with The Wall Street JournalвҖҷs Rolfe Winkler, the same reporter who got exclusive tours of Apple supplier facilities including TSMC, Foxconn, and GlobalWafers back in February.

 <https://substack.com/redirect/3ec9c017-858f-44c8-ae69-80fdd4756074?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 

To be clear, I am not dissing Winkler for getting an interview with Cook and being a conduit for AppleвҖҷs message. I have conversed with Winkler many times, he has quoted me in his pieces, and I find him to be a very diligent journalist. In fact, I know from first-hand experience that any interview with an Apple executive will yield little beyond the exact message Apple wants conveyed. 

And so it was in that interview <https://substack.com/redirect/be710e65-3377-4bec-b8a4-037db7835c8d?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  that Cook softened the ground for what would come next.

вҖңThe main point is unfortunately price increases are unavoidable. WeвҖҷre doing our best to mitigate the huge increases that are being passed to us,вҖқ Cook told the WSJ.

 <https://substack.com/redirect/2bb84cef-3bb3-48c4-90da-5bc0d6e09c83?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 

That point about mitigating price increases is not just PR fluff aimed at making us feel sorry for the company вҖ” although thatвҖҷs part of it. 

The truth is, my sources tell me, Apple has been pushing its supply chain hard over the past six to 12 months to save pennies and pounds. But the inflationary winds are stronger than even Mighty Apple can bear.


Eat the Cost


Apple could, theoretically, eat the cost and suffer lower margins as a result. But come on, itвҖҷs a publicly listed company whose $4 trillion value derives from a combination of cult-like following and the fattest margins in consumer electronics.

Within days of that WSJ interview came the price rise. And with it the blame game.

________________________________

Listen to Jon Y from Asianometry <https://substack.com/redirect/65910cde-0f2a-4cbd-9864-f0a4a5607459?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  and myself discuss how memory-makers ignored the coming AI boom in this episode of Supply-Chained. <https://substack.com/redirect/894ee676-a0bc-4d49-a13a-0d9047d14359?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 

 <https://substack.com/redirect/6e62e833-a3fb-407a-b390-eea03401b291?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 

Memory chips arenвҖҷt the only thing thatвҖҷs become more expensive over the past year. We already know that logic chips have gone up in price, which includes not only core A-series and M-series processors but also microcontrollers, power-management, networking, and display drivers. ThereвҖҷs also upward pressure on magnets, exterior casings, and even batteries.

But Cook chose to blame memory.

вҖңThereвҖҷs less supply at a time when consumers want devices and the memory guys are passing along huge price increases вҖҰ. We definitely need memory pricing and supply to return to reasonable levels for consumer products. ThatвҖҷs the bottom line.вҖқ вҖ” Apple CEO Tim Cook, WSJ 17 June 2026.

Memory takes the rap for two reasons. ItвҖҷs the component he has the least control over. More importantly, according to my discussions across the supply chain, memoryвҖҷs contribution to AppleвҖҷs total components costs will climb to as much as one-third of the bill of materials for many devices during calendar 2026. And Cook could do nothing to stop it.

WhatвҖҷs different about memory is AppleвҖҷs minuscule position in that market. Apple can, and has, bullied display makers into cutting OLED prices, keeping battery costs under control, and squeezing more out of camera-lens suppliers. ThatвҖҷs because in most cases itвҖҷs one of, if not the, largest customer. 

	 <https://substack.com/redirect/31791c7f-ce7f-4d7c-aabf-d9dbf91d7fb7?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 		
Image: Google Nano Banana

For dozens of companies the calculus is stark: lose Apple and you lose a huge slice of your business. The corporate graveyard is full of suppliers who once leant too heavily on Apple. If youвҖҷre lucky the company will buy you out <https://substack.com/redirect/15872511-0b7f-48bb-8f51-021ea3a806e9?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> . Unlucky and youвҖҷll face bankruptcy <https://substack.com/redirect/ee4072fc-8c93-4d01-a789-f5d4efb73a86?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> .

A major criticism leveled at Apple in the past week is that the company turned the screws on memory makers for years, and this price increase is its comeuppance. But thatвҖҷs not quite how it works, especially in DRAM.


Lack of Leverage


Apple has very little leverage over memory-chip makers. The company was once famous for buying up NAND because at the time the iPod was the single-largest use for this relatively expensive storage solution. Today, NAND is the primary storage in both laptops and smartphones, and Apple has a minor share <https://substack.com/redirect/037c2982-833c-46f7-9bb2-4a0e5746988e?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  in both.

Lenovo, Dell, and HP are the global leaders in personal computers, with each putting roughly the same amount of DRAM and NAND into their products as go into any flavor of Mac. And at roughly 20% global smartphone share, the iPhone has no monopsony power over Samsung, SK Hynix or Micron. ThereвҖҷs also the mammoth amounts of memory buzzing away in data centers across the globe dishing out Netflix shows, Spotify streams, and Substack feeds. 

As a result, Apple is nowhere near the largest buyer of DRAM and has a dwindling share of NAND purchases.

Memory makers will neither dine out nor go bankrupt on the basis of Apple orders. 

To the big three, Apple is just another customer. As proof, witness MicronвҖҷs recent announcement that it has signed <https://substack.com/redirect/7857bbf6-7a3e-43dd-a325-ed2eaf0b5da1?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  16 Strategic Customer Agreements (SCAs) вҖңacross the data center, consumer and auto market segments.вҖқ My sources tell me that cloud-service providers are a major component of this SCA roster, an indication that end-customers are getting more involved in the purchasing deals that were once left to manufacturing partners like Foxconn, Wistron, and Flex.


Crocodile Tears


Yet Micron is crying crocodile tears, referencing the last memory downturn as proof of its downtrodden status.

вҖңWe told a couple of the customers who were being very aggressive with pricing at that time that this is not constructive,вҖқ Micron Chief Business Officer Sumit Sadana told the WSJ <https://substack.com/redirect/ff991e71-7dd3-45ed-8976-2f3239dc8aae?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> . вҖңA lot of the industry investments got shut down in 2023 because of really poor pricing and really poor margins.вҖқ

Many, including the WSJ, figured Sadana was talking about Apple, even though he didnвҖҷt name the company directly.

I donвҖҷt know whom he was referencing, but thereвҖҷs a few things worth noting. The most obvious: he said вҖңa couple.вҖқ So, Apple isnвҖҷt solely responsible, if at all. Second, as I explained above, Apple simply does not have enough sway to depress MicronвҖҷs capex plans if thereвҖҷs enough market-wide demand to justify expansion.

Which brings me to another important point. While Micron recently signed over a dozen SCAs, Apple is also putting its own balance sheet to work by making advanced payments to the Boise, Idaho-based company to help its expansion in New York.

WhatвҖҷs more, Micron blaming the last downturn for failure to expand now is utterly nonsensical. ChatGPT came out in 2022 and within months it was clear that AI had ignited a computing-hardware boom. As early as May 2023, in a previous life, I wrote that Nvidia isnвҖҷt the only one getting a massive AI boost. <https://substack.com/redirect/f3411749-a0af-473e-ba3b-90642bd7e133?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc>  It was clear to me, a humble journalist, that memory demand was about to take off.

Comment, criticize, cheer? 

Share <https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=1582015&post_id=204383795&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=50datu&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjozMDI5NTE0NDIsInBvc3RfaWQiOjIwNDM4Mzc5NSwiaWF0IjoxNzgyOTA1Nzg1LCJleHAiOjE3ODU0OTc3ODUsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0xNTgyMDE1Iiwic3ViIjoicG9zdC1yZWFjdGlvbiJ9.Gb8pFZQzcESKDglaKXU81x4tU7hWYQOG2TRMz37B6Fo> 

To blame Apple, a minor buyer of a commodity product in the middle of a boom in which Apple has played almost no part reeks of hypocrisy, stupidity, and fear. Yes, fear.

As much as we all want to blame Apple, the truth is Micron and its fellow oligopolists are enjoying stratospheric price hikes without having made the capacity investments needed to meet the demand of an epoch-defining moment. That doesnвҖҷt make them downtrodden, that makes them antitrust targets.

At the same time, raising prices doesnвҖҷt help Apple sell more products in an era of tepid smartphone and PC growth. Many people want to label Apple a bully. ItвҖҷs certainly earnt that label for its actions over the past quarter century. But that was then, and weвҖҷre in a new era.

Apple didnвҖҷt raise prices because itвҖҷs a bully, it did so because itвҖҷs no longer a bully. In fact, Apple is starting to look like the opposite: an insecure kid struggling to remain relevant in a global supply chain thatвҖҷs found another schoolyard king.

Thanks for reading.

Satellites. Signals intelligence. Fleet trackers that do not care about anyoneвҖҷs feelings. Thirty years of people who lie to Congress but tell me the truth. Persian-language wires that print things the English-language press will not touch for another week. A hundred grand a year in research from firms whose clients do not fly commercial. Old Pappy 23. CLASSIFICATION: 


This used to cost the government a TS/SCI clearance <https://substack.com/redirect/fa71ea6c-76fc-46b0-900a-db409039c212?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> . They make you take a polygraph to get it. That is the governmentвҖҷs way of saying, we do not trust you, but we might need you, so first we are going to humiliate you and watch how you take it.

They sit you in a room with no windows. A man asks about your entire life. Who you slept with. What you took in college. And you tell him. And he does not react and he does not judge. He writes it down. In a government building in Virginia, with bad coffee and a machine measuring your sweat.

You wait a year to find out what happens.

Here is what they never tell you in that room. The classified part was never the war. The war was on television, between the truck commercials, in high definition, for four months. Everybody had that clearance. Everybody watched the bombs.

The classified part is the peace. The boring part. The part with no footage. The part where a war we won produces an Iran we cannot negotiate with, an oil market braced for the wrong catastrophe, and a Persian Gulf quietly changing landlords while the cameras pack up and drive home.

That is the part that used to cost a polygraph and a year of your life to read.

Now it costs $720 for a Substack subscription to Capital Mischief. 

The honesty is still required. But it is mine, not yours.

The newspapers sold you the apocalypse for four months and then quietly took it back, the way a man returns a tuxedo the Monday after the wedding.

World War Three. Two hundred dollar oil. The Strait of Hormuz in flames. The end of days, brought to you between an ad for erectile dysfunction medication and a pickup truck climbing a mountain for no discernible reason.

None of it happened.

Oil never got within a country mile of two hundred. The world declined to end. And the war that was supposed to be the story of the decade is, as I sit here, finished.

So let me tell you the story everybody missed while they stared at the horizon waiting for a mushroom cloud that had better things to do.

We won the war we could see. We are losing the war we cannot.

We assembled the largest naval armada since we invaded Iraq in 2003, and in a single night we killed the entire senior leadership of the Islamic Republic. 

That is the war we could see, and we won it the way a sledgehammer wins a disagreement with a soup tureen.

Then the thing underneath the leadership refused to die.

The Revolutionary Guard, an organization purpose-built to survive precisely the evening we had just delivered, climbed out of the rubble, dusted itself off, and did the one thing nobody at the Pentagon had war-gamed, sketched on a napkin, or considered over the third drink.

It took the country.

Iran lost the war and won the state. Write that on the back of your hand. Everything that follows is the footnote.

Now look at the clocks, because there are four of them and they are all running, and at least one of them is wired to something.

	 <https://substack.com/redirect/38a75547-3766-4847-99e2-a7881c6e5419?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 		

A ceasefire with an expiration date, negotiated at roughly the speed of a drive-through order, runs out on August 21st. 

The American midterms are coming, and for the first time in living memory an election calendar is a load-bearing input in a national security decision, which means our adversary does not actually have to defeat the United States. He only has to outlast a country with the attention span of a Labrador in a room full of tennis balls. 

The supreme leader of China comes to visit this fall. 

And underneath all three sits the only question still going to matter in 2030. What happens the morning after each of these clocks goes off.


This is the field guide. Seven chapters. 


They run downhill on purpose, from the penthouse of American power to the morgue of Iranian grand strategy, down through the weapon, the money, the throne, and the breadline, and back out into the daylight to shake hands with the landlord who inherits the whole building.

Here is where we are going.

Capital Mischief runs on subscribers, not sponsors, which is the only arrangement under which a man can afford to be honest. Free gets you in the door. Paid gets you the part I would have needed a security clearance to tell you a year ago. Either way, welcome.

Upgrade to paid <https://substack..ULQodNqS8MisibzdIt7-CMiW4XNMhzn5Pk1OwVhIVFo?&utm_medium=email&utm_source=subscribe-widget-preamble&utm_content=204371744> 

Chapter One. We won the war in one night, then immediately called timeout and started negotiating the rent on a tollbooth we could have simply taken.

Chapter Two. We keep complaining that Iran will not make a deal. The truth is worse and funnier. There is no longer an Iran to make a deal with.

Chapter Three. Iran field-tested a new weapon in the most important waterway on earth. It is not a missile. It is a convenience charge, and you cannot bomb a convenience charge.

Chapter Four. The oil trade nobody owns is not the spike. It is the crash. And the tollbooth takes its cut in yuan, which is the part that should ruin your weekend.

Chapter Five. Everybody is relieved the clergy got shoved aside. Everybody has it exactly backwards. The mullahs were the brakes.

Chapter Six. Six weeks before the war, Iranians were torching banks over the price of bread. Then we handed the regime the one thing no police state can manufacture for itself. A foreign enemy.

Chapter Seven. The question was never Iran. It is who owns the neighborhood now that the old landlord is dead, and why the new one signs his name in Mandarin.

	 <https://substack.com/redirect/752c92bd-2f03-4c44-9b0d-02687e5a3127?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 		

Pour the coffee. Light something.

This one runs long, and unlike the apocalypse, it is real.


Chapter One: We Built the Biggest Navy Since 2003 to Win a War, and Now We Are Negotiating the Rent on a Tollbooth We Could Have Taken for Free


Let me describe the single most impressive thing the United States Navy has done since it rolled into Baghdad.

Three carrier strike groups. B-2 bombers strolling over nuclear sites the brochures swore were untouchable. A naval blockade the newspapers correctly billed as the largest since we drew a chalk line around Cuba in 1962.

At the peak, roughly four of every ten deployed warships on planet Earth were bobbing in a single puddle the size of a generous Midwestern lake.

It was magnificent. For about six weeks the old country looked like it had remembered where it left its keys.

And then, at the exact moment the job was within armвҖҷs reach, we called a sixty-day timeout.

I want to explain what a timeout is, in the only dialect spoken at a chokepoint, because Washington appears to have misplaced the phrasebook.

Deterrence is not a demonstration of what you can do. Everybody already knows what you can do. They watched it on television, between the truck commercials. 

Deterrence is a demonstration of whether you will finish, and a timeout is the formal announcement, notarized and read aloud, that your willingness to finish has gone home for the weekend.

The enemy reads that announcement perfectly. He does not even need the translator.

Which is why the Revolutionary Guard has spent every single day of this ceasefire doing one thing with the grim focus of a man assembling Swedish furniture against a deadline. Tightening its grip on the Iranian state.

We did not pause the war. We gave the loser a paid sabbatical to go win the peace.

And here is the monument to it, poured in concrete, no interpretation required.

The single most valuable piece of real estate in the global economy, the channel through which a fifth of the worldвҖҷs oil has to physically squeeze, is now operated by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps as a tollbooth. 

	 <https://substack.com/redirect/108961fe-20ec-4c55-a60c-9f003ab561d0?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 		

They inspect the cars. They set the fare. Somewhere between a million and a half and two million dollars a vehicle, and they are not validating parking and they are not taking American Express.

And the government of the United States is at the booth, in the rain, asking about the monthly rate.

That is the case against the timeout you already half-believe, and it is correct as far as it walks. You do not pause a won war. You finish it. 

A ceasefire that lets the loser reassemble his command, bolt the doors of the state from the inside, and franchise the chokepoint is not a ceasefire. It is a wire transfer, denominated in the one currency a superpower cannot print, which is time. 

We learned this lesson in person in the marshes of southern Iraq in 1991, filed it under Miscellaneous, and lost the folder.

But that is not the real story. The real story is that adorable little number the President keeps handing to anyone holding a microphone.


Fifty-fifty.


Everybody hears leverage. The dealmaker keeping his cards face down. The calculated maybe. The art of the thing.

I hear a doctrine dying in real time, on camera, in flattering light.

Go find me the moment in the actual unipolar era when an American president stood at a lectern and mused that it was a coin flip whether he would enforce his own red line. You cannot find it, because it does not exist. 

When we were genuinely the only adult in the room, there was no fifty-fifty. There was the demand, and there was the enforcement, and the interval between them was measured in the time it takes a carrier to turn into the wind.

The public agonizing is the tell.

It tells you the enforcement is no longer reflexive. It tells you the chokepoint is in the other guyвҖҷs fist. It tells you the price of oil now runs through a warehouse in China we do not have the keys to. 

And it tells you the November midterms are a variable in a national security equation, which means our adversary does not have to out-fight us. He has to out-wait us, and he is up against an electorate that reconsiders its entire worldview every time gasoline moves a dime.

A superpower that has to visibly agonize over whether to enforce its own red line has already informed the planet that the red line is for sale.

That is the whole game, in three syllables. Not a tactic. A status report on American primacy, read aloud by the man holding the office, who may not have noticed he is reading it.

So here is what to watch, because I do not trade on feelings, I trade on indicators.

Watch the carriers. If a strike group sails home and nobody takes its parking spot before the August clock runs out, that is the tell that Washington has privately signed the deal and is now just managing the lighting. 

Weigh the hard movement. Escort screens, Suez transits, the fleet trackers that do not care about anyoneвҖҷs feelings. Ignore the press releases about morale and shore leave. Those are written for you, not for me.

Watch whether the tollbooth gets bolted down permanently under IranвҖҷs Supreme National Security Council. If it does, the strait is no longer a wartime improvisation. It is a fixture, and we installed it ourselves, by blinking.

And watch the number. The day fifty-fifty quietly becomes seventy-thirty in either direction, the decision was made a while ago and somebody is lying about the timestamp.

We had the power to take the chokepoint.

We elected to negotiate the fare.

Remember which of those is a victory, and which is the architecture of decline wearing a good suit and answering to the name Prudence.


Chapter Two: We Blew Up the Switchboard and Now We Are Furious That Nobody Picks Up


We had the power to take the chokepoint. We chose to pay the rent. And to understand why we are paying rent on anything, you have to understand what we blew up the night we won. We did not just kill a leadership. We killed the only thing in Iran that could ever have said yes.

For forty-six years the Islamic Republic of Iran had one feature that made it possible to threaten, to bargain with, and to deter.

It had an address.

The Supreme Leader was paranoid, rigid, and hostile to everything we hold dear, including indoor plumbing for women. Fine. But he was a single mind. 

You could deliver a threat to that mind, that mind could weigh it against the survival of the regime, and that mind could say yes and make the yes stick all the way down the ladder.

That is the entire mechanical basis of coercion. 

You need a someone. 

You need a human being who can sign, and who can make the people below him honor the signature.

On February the twenty-eighth, we found that someone and we killed him.

	 <https://substack.com/redirect/0b3eb92c-767e-4294-b3d4-2238203b2f43?j=eyJ1IjoiNTBkYXR1In0.wMhVDxPSNfOaCS4gLOy6QmLs4ijRPD8hP3NmO4HbRXc> 		

Sit with the irony, because it is the deepest one of the whole war and the entire press corps strolled right past it on the way to the buffet. We wanted leverage. 

Leverage is a thing you apply to a decision-maker. And we applied two thousand pounds of it directly to his house.

You do not acquire leverage by destroying the office that receives it. You acquire a vacancy.

So when you watch the negotiations grind toward the August deadline, understand what you are actually watching. You are not watching whether Iran will accept terms. 

You are watching whether anyone left breathing in Iran has the authority to accept terms and then enforce them on a security apparatus that was specifically engineered to keep fighting after the top of the building is gone.

The Foreign Minister can sign anything you slide across the table. He controls nothing. We learned this in April, in real time, when he announced the strait was open and the Guard reversed him before he finished his coffee. 

That was not a mixed signal out of Tehran. That was the signal. The diplomat is the customer service desk. The Guard is management, and management does not come to the phone.

Now here is the part most people have never had laid out cleanly, and it is true.

Every bomb we dropped confirmed the prophecy.

The founding belief of this regime, since 1979, is that the outside world has always wanted to destroy it and will never, under any terms, accept it. For forty-six years that was paranoia. It was the line the regime fed its people to keep them frightened, obedient, and indoors.

On February the twenty-eighth, the paranoia got promoted to prophecy. And vindicated paranoia is the single most dangerous thing a regime can believe, because it deletes every incentive to compromise and rewards every instinct to dig the bunker deeper.

We were promised that pressure would peel the war-weary public off the hardliners. Instead pressure proved the hardliners right, in front of that public, on the evening news, in high definition. 

The maximum-pressure faith has been producing this exact result for thirty years and acting surprised every single time, which is the working definition of a man who keeps stapling his own tie to the desk.

That is the part you half knew. Here is the part nobody is writing.

Iran no longer has a foreign policy. It has thirty-one of them.

The death of Iranian grand strategy is the most important geopolitical event of the decade, and it does not even have a name yet, because everyone in Washington is still modeling Iran the old way. 

As a unitary rational actor. A country that wants things in the singular and makes cost-benefit calculations a war college can draw on a whiteboard.

That model is now scrap, and the people using it have not been told.

There is no longer an Iran that wants things in the singular. There is a Revolutionary Guard that wants to survive. There is a clerical class that has been moved to the kidsвҖҷ table. There is a wounded symbol on a throne who has never once been filmed. 

And there are thirty-one provincial commands that were each built, on purpose, to calculate locally and fight alone.

You are not negotiating with a country. You are negotiating with a hive that has lost its queen and is running on muscle memory.

And here is why that is worse than a hostile state, not better.

A hostile state can be deterred, because it has a center that wants to keep breathing. 

A hive cannot. A hive does not weigh your threat. A hive does not lie awake fearing extinction the way a man does at three in the morning. A hive simply keeps doing the thing it was built to do until the weather changes.

We spent the better part of a year and a fortune in precision ordnance hunting the queen, and we got her. And in getting her we did not end the swarm. We released it from the one mind that could have called it home.

So the indicators I am watching are not about whether a deal gets signed. Anybody can sign. A signature is just a man being polite with a pen. I am watching whether a signature means anything.

Watch for a single Iranian authority that can issue an order and have it obeyed in more than one province at once. If you cannot find one, there is no deal. There is a document, suitable for framing.

Watch the Foreign Minister get overruled again. The next time he says something reasonable and the Guard contradicts him inside forty-eight hours, that is your proof the switchboard is still dead and the polite man with the pen was negotiating on behalf of nobody.

We blew up the phone.

Now we are standing in the rubble, holding the receiver, furious that nobody is answering, and calling it Iranian intransigence.

It is not intransigence. There is simply no one home to be rude to us.

Here's the deal. I don't work for an oil company, a network, or a guy in a suit in Washington. I work for the people who subscribe. Free tier, welcome aboard. Paid tier, now we're talking. Everybody else is reading the news. You're reading me.
