emails.subject: UBS: UBS Global I/O Semiconductors "Cloud AI: TSMC and ASE driving faster CoWoS..."
emails.body:
Adv packaging for next-gen AI chips diversifying, but TSMC remains the leader with a 50% sales CAGR for the segment the next 5 years. 
 <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed83> 	 	 Global Research 
01 July 2026 	
Global I/O	в—Ҹ	UBS Global I/O Semiconductors	
Cloud AI: TSMC and ASE driving faster CoWoS expansion, unlocking larger TAM <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed84&e=ZGlkPUFDNy1FLTYxOTIwMDIyJm9mZl9pZD1BQzctRS0xNTg5NTgyMjM0Jm1hPVk0Njc4NTM0OTQ5Nzg1QTQ1JmNhbXBfaWQ9RU06VU5LVzoyMDI2LTA3OjAxOkM&s=fBX7HTilmP8e3PuTRpl0DUVq1zH98KoYbaFOvMDWu5A>  	
Adv packaging for next-gen AI chips diversifying, but TSMC remains the leader with a 50% sales CAGR for the segment the next 5 years. 


Read Report   > <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed85&e=ZGlkPUFDNy1FLTYxOTIwMDIyJm9mZl9pZD1BQzctRS0xNTg5NTgyMjM1Jm1hPVk0Njc4NTM0OTQ5Nzg1QTQ1JmNhbXBfaWQ9RU06VU5LVzoyMDI2LTA3OjAxOkM&s=hC4t2pTa1-P1uMT9SKqtGMJlI2qIs2crA-O3WHKk9UA> 		
CoWoS capacity upside in 2026-27 across TSMC and ASE 

We view TSMC and broader industry CoWoS expansion as a leading indicator of cloud AI demand over the next 2-3 years. Capacity expansion over the next 12-18 months is progressing faster than we expected a month ago <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed86&e=ZGlkPUFDNy1FLTYxOTIwMDIyJm9mZl9pZD1BQzctRS0xNTg5NTgyMjM2Jm1hPVk0Njc4NTM0OTQ5Nzg1QTQ1JmNhbXBfaWQ9RU06VU5LVzoyMDI2LTA3OjAxOkM&s=xu2nr_955ZiTJ_IxOTRJR2-egtz0l100I4z2-4mzUjw> , suggesting stronger underlying cloud AI demand. TSMC's CoWoS capacity expansion is re-accelerating and we now expect capacity to reach 130k/180k wpm by end-2026/27 vs our prior estimates of 120k / 150k wpm. We believe this reflects a much larger advanced packaging TAM over the next several years, with a meaningful portion likely to remain on CoWoS even as Intel EMIB-T and TSMC CoPoS enter mass production in 2028. ASE has also been accelerating its full-process capacity expansion for 2027. We now forecast its full-process CoWoS capacity to more than double from 20kwpm at end-2026E to ~50kwpm end-2027E vs prior estimate of 40kwpm. (See our ASE note <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed87&e=ZGlkPUFDNy1FLTYxOTIwMDIyJm9mZl9pZD1BQzctRS0xNTg5NTgyMjM3Jm1hPVk0Njc4NTM0OTQ5Nzg1QTQ1JmNhbXBfaWQ9RU06VU5LVzoyMDI2LTA3OjAxOkM&s=3auZgngxB5NczXvN40g8hUJaAHT3_0OrLxHSAv7E68M> ). Overall, we estimate industry CoWoS capacity may rise from 160kwpm at end-2026 to 250kwpm by end-2027. 

Robust server CPU demand; Nvidia Rubin ramp to accelerate in H226 

On the demand side, we believe server CPU continues to show upside into 2027. ASE's faster CoWoS expansion should be underpinned by higher AMD Venice volumes, which we estimate at 4mn units in 2027, up from 1.3mn in 2026. AMD's CoWoS demand could grow 232% in 2027E with a strong ramp of Venice server CPU and MI450/455. We also lift Nvidia's Vera CPU units to 5.5mn units in 2027 from 1.6mn in 2026, implying 57% growth in Nvidia's CoWoS demand in 2027. Rubin GPU ramp was slower in Q226 due to redesigns, but we anticipate a steep pick up in H226 to reach 2.1mn for 2026. We forecast Google TPU chipset units to rise from 4.1mn in 2026 to 9.0mn in 2027, with MediaTek expected to support 4mn units of TPU v8t capacity. Amazon Trainium 3 demand is growing and we lift 2026/27E unit estimates to 1.8mn/2.8mn (from 1.7m/2.3m). See our CoWoS supply vs demand analysis in Figure 2-Figure 12. 

More diversified supply ahead, but TSMC should remain the largest player 

As previously noted <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed88&e=ZGlkPUFDNy1FLTYxOTIwMDIyJm9mZl9pZD1BQzctRS0xNTg5NTgyMjM4Jm1hPVk0Njc4NTM0OTQ5Nzg1QTQ1JmNhbXBfaWQ9RU06VU5LVzoyMDI2LTA3OjAxOkM&s=1S6_XqvhUvFE9KtZ1dMqJFnakX1llY-Bx2u8kWoytc0> , we anticipate advanced packaging supply to become more diversified from 2027-28 onward. We see increasing opportunities for ASE and Amkor in H226/2027, mainly driven by server CPUs. Intel's EMIB-T is gaining traction, but we believe Intel may face resource and capacity constraints in 2028, and will likely prioritise internal products and Google/MediaTek's TPU v9 initially. Overall, we think TSMC's advanced packaging sales should sustain solid ~50% growth over the next five years on its leading market share, rising content through 3D stacking, and tech upgrades such as CoPoS and co-packaged optics. 

Stock recommendations; ASE/GPTC price target raised to NT$835/$5,000 

Along the semiconductor supply chain for cloud AI, our top picks are TSMC (the industry's leading cloud AI foundry), MediaTek (design services for Google TPU), and ASE (advanced packaging & testing). We estimate backend, including advanced packaging, to reach ~12%/15% of TSMC sales in 2026/27E, and our recent Q226 earnings preview <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed89&e=ZGlkPUFDNy1FLTYxOTIwMDIyJm9mZl9pZD1BQzctRS0xNTg5NTgyMjM5Jm1hPVk0Njc4NTM0OTQ5Nzg1QTQ1JmNhbXBfaWQ9RU06VU5LVzoyMDI2LTA3OjAxOkM&s=-3kF-SqnABCq9z6VwHTgtDOMOZh1TmMmSIJD-WSh9kE> raised our sales and EPS estimates on stronger cloud AI demand. MediaTek is a Buy and a Key Call. We lift our ASE price target to NT$835 from NT$660 <https://neo.ubs.com/r/7/?id=h798f8806,3b0d316,4be1ed8a&e=ZGlkPUFDNy1FLTYxOTIwMDIyJm9mZl9pZD1BQzctRS0xNTg5NTgyMjQwJm1hPVk0Njc4NTM0OTQ5Nzg1QTQ1JmNhbXBfaWQ9RU06VU5LVzoyMDI2LTA3OjAxOkM&s=lxVV3Cgc1oL1-HUBuWg60XA89-agFPIQK3A6DL65zUY> . Critical equipment suppliers for advanced packaging & testing could also benefit from the cloud AI ramp-up, and we like GPTC, Chroma, Hon Precision, and ASMPT. We raise our GPTC price target to NT$5,000 (from NT$4,000) based on 33x 2027-28E PE (vs 33x 2027E PE previously). See page 6. KYEC remains well positioned in the final test space. We like Aspeed for its strong BMC outlook, GUC for its robust Google CPU upside potential, and Alchip for its Amazon Trainium3 and Trainium4 opportunities.

emails.subject: Semiconductors: Semiconductor Inventory Tracker: Not Restocking Yet
emails.body:
Semiconductors 	
Semiconductor Inventory Tracker: Not Restocking Yet	 
Read Full Report <https://ms.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13515781&ad=4538214017&h=DPgazJ6&sig=TR48zAL7WkrV73hRAzWEj3ifPGy&v=2&url=https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/27302d02-6f5d-11f1-b8bb-85ba1f0ac5ae?t%3D1782879381:1922:17494:acba1c0882e9%26m%3D1%26ch%3DCDF%20Research%20ISG%20Autoblast%23e%3DdHJhY2tlZC1tb29yZWpvcy1jZGZhLTMwMzQ4NzktcGNkZmlyZXMtcG1ocGxvY3ZibGJ6K3d2bTB5YW92QG1zLnN0cmVldGNvbnR4dC5uZXQ%3D> 	 
(PDF: 16 Pages) 	
North America 	
Industry View   Attractive

Joseph Moore, Nicole Kozhukhov, Ella Tulchinsky, Shane Brett, Mason Wayne	 
July 1, 2026

	
Supply chain inventory rose q/q but outperformed seasonality, with below-seasonal producer builds and distributor declines. Supply is tightening, but meaningful restocking has yet to emerge. 
Key takeaways	 

*	Overall supply chain outperformed seasonality, increasing by less than the typical 1Q increase. 
*	Producer and disti DOIs outperformed seasonality; customers were ~in-line. 
*	Auto OEM DOI moved above historical median, while Auto Supplier DOI declined to in-line with historical levels. 

Overall supply chain inventory increased q/q in 1Q, but the build was below typical 1Q seasonality. We remain above historical median levels, as distributors declined and producers built less than seasonal levels, while customers were ~in-line. While this is a step in the right direction, we still remain 33 days above historical median for the supply chain, and have not yet seen a broader restocking cycle.

The quarter reflected a mix of normal 1Q inventory build and continued discipline across the supply chain rather than broad restocking. Distributors continue to lean out, producer builds remain relatively steady, and customer trends were mixed. For stocks, we prefer areas where supply/demand is tightening or inventory risk is more defensible, including premium-end analog exposure in ADI and NXP, compute/networking names NVDA, AVGO, and CBRS, memory names MU and SNDK, and semicap names MKSI, KLAC, LRCX and ONTO.

Stacked DOI increased 9 days vs. a seasonal 19-day increase and is 33 days above the historical median. Trailing decreased ~2 days. 
 
Source: FactSet, Morgan Stanley Research 
Read more... <https://ms.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13515781&ad=4538214017&h=DPgazJ6&sig=TR48zAL7WkrV73hRAzWEj3ifPGy&v=2&url=https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/27302d02-6f5d-11f1-b8bb-85ba1f0ac5ae?t%3D1782879381:1922:17494:acba1c0882e9%26m%3D1%26ch%3DCDF%20Research%20ISG%20Autoblast%23e%3DdHJhY2tlZC1tb29yZWpvcy1jZGZhLTMwMzQ4NzktcGNkZmlyZXMtcG1ocGxvY3ZibGJ6K3d2bTB5YW92QG1zLnN0cmVldGNvbnR4dC5uZXQ%3D>

emails.subject: IDEA: Grindr Inc. (GRND.N): On the EDGE of Glory; Upgrade to Overweight
emails.body:
Grindr Inc. (GRND.N) 	
On the EDGE of Glory; Upgrade to Overweight	 
Read Full Report <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/09d5dbfc-7184-11f1-b8bb-85ba1f0ac5ae?t=1782878682:3075:45476:5e2e4f67363b&m=1&ch=CDF Research ISG#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==> 	 
(PDF: 45 Pages) 	
North America 	
Stock Rating   Overweight
Price Target   $18.00

Nathan Feather, Brian Nowak, CFA	 
July 1, 2026

	
WHAT'S CHANGED?	 
Grindr Inc. (GRND.N)	 
Rating	 
From:   Equal-weight	 To: Overweight	 
Price Target	 
From:   $15.00	 To: $18.00	 
GRND is a one-of-one asset thatвҖҷs heavily undermonetized versus peers, but it's launching two products which could change that: an ultra-premium subscription ($100-500/mo.) and a DTC telehealth brand. Trading at a ~35% discount to peers with a compelling catalyst path, we upgrade to OW; $18 PT. 
Key takeaways	 

*	GRND has strong fundamentals with a now compelling catalyst path trading at a discounted valuation; Upgrade to Overweight 
*	A high quality asset with strong network effects (15M MAUs), industry-leading engagement (>65 mins/DAU), and significant profitability (>40% EBITDA margin) 
*	GRND monetizes ~30% below peers, but it's launching 2 products which could help: EDGE (ultra-premium tier coming 4Q/1Q) and Woodwork (DTC telehealth brand) 
*	With GRND's revenue growth of ~$90M/year (on a ~$500M base), we believe EDGE and Woodwork each have the potential to deliver 'one-year' or more of growth 
*	Its multiple has ~halved over the past year to ~11x EBITDA, or a ~35% growth-adjusted discount to peers, creating a compelling entry point 

High quality asset, a compelling catalyst path, and discounted valuation; upgrade to OW: Grindr is an underappreciated company with strong network effects, industry-leading engagement, and significant profitability. However, the stock is down 36% over the past year on concerns that paywall-led monetization was impacting user growth. We initially shared those concerns <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/fb1decd2-8fe1-11f0-a3f6-3da67634994d?ch=rp&sch=cr> , but have grown meaningfully more constructive as GRND has pivoted towards healthier product-led monetization with the upcoming launch of its ultra-premium tier EDGE (~$100-$500/mo.) and telehealth brand Woodwork (a HIMS competitor). Either could be individually transformative for GRND, but with both in development we now have conviction the brand can grow revenue at a 18% '25-'28 CAGRвҖҰ with far more upside than downside. With the stock trading at just 11x '27 EBITDA, a ~35% discount to peers on a growth-adjusted basis, we see a compelling entry point ahead of a rich catalyst path and upgrade to OW with a $18 price target (25% upside). If EDGE or Woodwork can become a meaningful win for the brand, we believe our $29 bull case valuation could be in play (~100% upside). 

GRND is a one-of-one asset thatвҖҷs heavily undermonetized versus peers: Grindr has strong network effects among its 15M users, reaching 60% global brand awareness despite marketing spend at <4% of revenue. Users are highly engaged with DAUs spending >65 minutes on the app daily. That has translated to consistent >40% EBITDA margins. For a sense of its ubiquity, about half of gay relationships in the US start on GRND. It also plays in a higher income and more retentive segment than online dating brands like Tinder or Bumble. Despite its advantages, the app monetizes ~30% below other dating apps - a gap which we believe can close as GRND expands its product suite. 

GRND is shifting from value extraction to value creation with two products that could enable step-function upside: Grindr had been underinvested in and undermonetized before going public in 2022. Since then, the company materially increased and optimized the paywall which delivered a remarkable ~31% '22-'25 topline CAGR, but also increased user frustration and led to MAU growth concerns. The company has been working to pivot towards healthier product-led monetization (creating new value for users and then capturing it). That process has taken time, but we now have line of sight to meaningful contribution from two new products that we believe can drive revenue without impacting user growth: EDGE (an ultra-premium tier) and Woodwork (a DTC telehealth brand). The upside from these new products is material: GRND has grown revenue at ~$90M per year and we believe EDGE and Woodwork could each deliver 'one-year' or more of revenue growth (>$90M).

1) EDGE: Grindr is set to launch an ultra-premium, AI-led tier which could drive the next leg of growth: GRND is planning to launch EDGE in 4Q26/1Q27, targeting its high-income power users with AI-based tools for better profile discovery and ways to re-spark past connections. The company tested pricing at $80/month in Australia and saw significantly higher than anticipated demand. As a result, it's now testing pricing at $100-$500/month. That's well above anything major peers are charging and could represent a step-change in revenue with even modest adoption. For every 10bps of payer penetration at $100/month, EDGE would be a 4% contribution to revenue. So GRND getting 0.25% of its MAUs to subscribe to EDGE at $200/mo. would be equivalent to an entire year of revenue growth (~$95M)вҖҰ and GRND is targeting higher payer penetration and pricing(~50-100bps with pricing up to $500/mo.). In our base case, we model EDGE generates $78M of revenue by '28 (11% of total) while in our bull case we model $113M (15% of bull revenue).

2) Woodwork: Its new telehealth business offers a novel physical + digital bundle: GRND is also planning to launch a variety of DTC product lines to sell to its users. The first is Woodwork, a HIMS-like telehealth business offering ED medication, GLP-1s, and peptides (HIMS is covered by Craig Hettenbach). As 30% of its user base uses ED medication and 60% have considered it, there is a sizeable addressable market. The value prop is clear as Woodwork bundles a free Grindr subscription with competitively priced medication. As <10% of users currently pay, this could help the brand monetize an entirely new cohort. Woodwork is off to a strong start as web traffic is now >1/3rd of hims.com in the US despite <$10M of marketing spend. The upside is material: for every 1% of its US MAUs it can cross-sell, we estimate Woodwork will generate ~2-4pts of revenue growth. That means GRND getting 5% of its US MAUs to subscribe to Woodwork would be equivalent to a year of revenue growth (~$95M, assuming ~75% are new subs). In our base case, we model Woodwork generates $28M of revenue by '28 (4% of total) while in our bull case we model $52M (7% of bull revenue).

Taken together, we now project a 16% '26-'28 revenue CAGR with multiple levers for upside: With increased conviction in contribution from EDGE and Woodwork, we raise our '26/'27 revenue growth estimates to 23%/17% y/y. Although EDGE and Woodwork are new segments, we believe our revenue estimates are highly achievable with clear paths for upside. A few ways to contextualize this. We forecast EDGE and Woodwork contribute ~9pts to the '26-'28 revenue CAGR or ~60% of growth. With ~4% MAU growth, that means you only need to assume ~2pts of growth from other initiatives to reach our base caseвҖҰ which likely proves conservative. This implies Grindr earns ~$3.65 per MAU in '28 which is in-line with what Tinder earns today & still ~7% below Hinge. Other revenue levers could be pulled as well, such as advertising where GRND currently monetizes ~2-4x below other mobile apps despite its higher-income demographic. And if EDGE and Woodwork both fail to gain adoption, GRND could further optimize the paywall to drive revenue.

GRND's valuation is attractive at 11x '27 EBITDA, a compelling entry point: GRND's FY2 EV/EBITDA multiple has roughly halved from ~20x last July to ~11x today (well below its 15x 2-year avg.) as concerns on MAU growth and upside to estimates combined with tactical selling. We believe the primary drivers behind the de-rating are largely reversing which could support multiple expansion from here. As a result, GRND trades at a ~35% discount to consumer subscription and freemium peers on a growth-adjusted basis. Our updated $18 price target shows 25% upside even with modest contribution from new growth initiatives while our $29 bull case valuation implies ~100% upside if EDGE and Woodwork can succeed. With a limited floor and multiple positive call options, we see an asymmetric return profile with a 2:1 bull-bear skew. Key catalysts to watch are the EDGE launch in 4Q26/1Q27, incremental Woodwork disclosure (2H26/1H27), and further beat and raises.

Risks to our thesis: 1) Competition. Although GRND has a dominant share within male-for-male dating apps, we've seen rising competition from web-based competitors including a recent investment in the space by MTCH which could incrementally pressure MAUs/sentiment. 2) EDGE/Woodwork struggle to gain adoption. Both these programs are novel ways of monetizing and could generate less revenue than we expect. That would lead to either slower topline growth or, more likely, a return to more value extractive monetization. 3) Lack of beat and raises in 2H26. As it starts to lap the price increase, GRND's revenue growth will decelerate in 2H26. We think that is priced in, but if it doesn't beat and raise the market may question the sustainable rate of growth.

We raise our revenue/EBITDA estimates by ~1%/~2% in '26 and '27, PT goes to $18 from $15. We raise our revenue estimates by ~1% each in '26 and '27 to reflect ramping contributions from EDGE and Woodwork (we estimate $43M/$10M in '27, respectively). With operating leverage, this leads to a ~2% increase in EBITDA over both '26 and '27. Our DCF-driven PT increases from $15 to $18 as we gain incremental confidence in medium-term growth prospects and a lower WACC.

For our full Overweight pitch, see the slide deck in the note. Read more... <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/09d5dbfc-7184-11f1-b8bb-85ba1f0ac5ae?t=1782878682:3075:45476:5e2e4f67363b&m=1&ch=CDF Research ISG#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==>

emails.subject: USMCA Negotiations vol. 2 - Where Things Stand Now On July 1
emails.body:
<https://www.jefferies.com>   	Equity Research
July 1, 2026	 
	Canada | Cross-Sector Views	 
 <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/links2/html/40f635e3-d597-4108-9b84-1719b7b8f9e5> 	 	 
USMCA Negotiations vol. 2 - Where Things Stand Now On July 1 <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/links2/html/40f635e3-d597-4108-9b84-1719b7b8f9e5> 	 
The mandatory July 1 USMCA joint review shifted from a routine assessment into a high-stakes renegotiation under Trump 2.0. Given contentious U.S.вҖ“Canada talks, the two economies now face ambiguity without the formal renewal, moving to annual reviews with the U.S. pursuing bilateral carve-outs. Trade keeps flowing, but policy uncertainty is extended indefinitely. While negative for the economic outlook, it is preferable to the devastation of a full withdrawal.

	
Source: Jefferies, Governments of Canada, Mexico and the U.S.	

As we have now passed the July 1 deadline to renew the USMCA, the agreement has now devolved into 10 years of annual reviews until the agreement renegotiated or expires (unlikely). While President Trump recently disparaged the agreement that he negotiated six years ago, it is unlikely that the U.S. will terminate the agreement. However, even with progress between Mexico and the U.S., we see no immediate resolution between Canada and the U.S., with pundits going as far as saying that discussions could proceed past the November mid-terms. We do not believe the market is adequately pricing in the risk of extended negotiations heading into the winter or even 2027.

While trade continues to flow between Canada and the U.S., the lack of certainty has some specific impacts on the economy that will remain as headwinds until there is greater clarity:

*	Prolonged Investment Limbo: A rolling annual review cycle effectively institutionalizes a state of permanent trade negotiation and policy uncertainty, keeping businesses and multinational corporations in a state of long-term planning limbo.
*	Chilled Capital Expenditure: While trade continues under existing tariff-free frameworks, the lack of long-term regulatory visibility is expected to chill capital investment, delay major corporate relocations (nearshoring), and compress operating margins for cross-border logistics.

From our perspective, the negotiating stances between the two nations remain far apart with little movement in recent discussions. The U.S. remains frustrated with Canada's supply management in its dairy industry, provincial bans on American alcohol sales and has pressured the Canadian government on its digital streaming tax.

Removing steel & aluminum tariffs is a priority for Canada. For the Canadian metals and mining sector, a key goal for Canada in its negotiations is removing the 50% tariffs on steel and aluminum exports to the US. The next best outcome would be reducing the tariff rate on steel and aluminum - potentially to 25% on steel and 10% on aluminum, as was the case briefly in 2018. We think this is more likely than 0% tariffs. It appears bilateral agreements, instead of a renewal of USMCA, is the most likely outcome, in which case applying different tariff rates to different metals is certainly on the table. A bilateral agreement would represent a more formal framework - one that is adjacent to USMCA - vs. the current piecemeal Section 232 tariffs. We think it's unlikely tariffs will be applied to other metals such as copper and gold in a bilateral agreement. Finally, we expect Canada will use critical minerals supply as leverage to negotiate lower tariffs on steel and aluminum.

Other Publications in and related to the series:

*	USMCA Negotiations - Implications for the Canadian Economy and Major Sectors <https://content.jefferies.com/links2/pdf/6aab565b-4fb0-436f-8007-eb813b570270?firmId=32195> , April 16
*	USMCA Negotiations: Implications for Canadian Economy & Major Sectors | REPLAY <https://content.jefferies.com/links2/pdf/295f9e39-143b-4cde-8be0-a23d0c872cdb?firmId=32195> , April 27
*	Unpacking USMCA Review with The US Chamber of Commerce | RECAP <https://content.jefferies.com/links2/pdf/57011018-2df5-443b-88be-638e7399ce68?firmId=32195> , May 15
*	USMCA Outlook with Fm. Undersecretary of Finance of Mexico | REPLAY <https://content.jefferies.com/links2/pdf/80b08194-ef1c-4406-aac2-a12409f6628d?firmId=32195> , June 1

emails.subject: Alcoa Corp (AA.N): Sizing Up With S32 Assets; Accretive On Citi's Bullish Price Deck.
emails.body:
Alcoa has announced the acquisition of S32's bauxite, alumina and aluminum assets (ex-Mozal).  Payment is mostly cash, i.e. long-term success/failure will largely be a function on aluminum/a prices.  The deal adds more alumina (+54%) than aluminum (+34%).  The transaction is not a surprise - Alcoa mgmt. had flagged its appetite for this kind of M&A with asset overlap in Australia and Brazil.  Deal size is $4.7-5.4bn EV (with contingent payments) or 31-36% of AA.  S32 is flagging $1.2bn of ARO's that are less at ~$400m under US GAAP.  Valuation isn't straightforward given huge recent volatility in aluminum/a prices but is EBITDA per share accretive on 2025, 2026E, 2027E and Spot prices - see Fig 1.    AA's stock may see pressure & the stock component is a future overhang - but the numbers work on Citi's bullish price deck & we like the clear path to de-leveraging (FCF, asset sales, Ma'aden stock sales).   
 	
Flash | Citi Research 	
Alcoa Corp (AA.N) | Buy 
Sizing Up With S32 Assets; Accretive On Citi's Bullish Price Deck. 
CITI'S TAKE 

Alcoa has announced the acquisition of S32's bauxite, alumina and aluminum assets (ex-Mozal).  Payment is mostly cash, i.e. long-term success/failure will largely be a function on aluminum/a prices.  The deal adds more alumina (+54%) than aluminum (+34%).  The transaction is not a surprise - Alcoa mgmt. had flagged its appetite for this kind of M&A with asset overlap in Australia and Brazil.  Deal size is $4.7-5.4bn EV (with contingent payments) or 31-36% of AA.  S32 is flagging $1.2bn of ARO's that are less at ~$400m under US GAAP.  Valuation isn't straightforward given huge recent volatility in aluminum/a prices but is EBITDA per share accretive on 2025, 2026E, 2027E and Spot prices - see Fig 1.    AA's stock may see pressure & the stock component is a future overhang - but the numbers work on Citi's bullish price deck & we like the clear path to de-leveraging (FCF, asset sales, Ma'aden stock sales).  

	

Highlights from the AA call:

*	Management rationale: Clear strategic fit - highly complementary assets.   Synergies - $900m NPV,  including $50m in 1 year run-rate cost savings.  Note: the vast majority synergies are in Western Australia mining/refinery operations.  Financials - EPS and FCF accretive.   AA is acquiring well below replacement costs: $1,850/ton smelting; $600/ton refining.
*	Asset highlights: Western Australia - Boddington bauxite & Worsley alumina refinery:  big opportunity to optimize long-term mine planning.   Brazil - Additional interest in Alumar refinery & smelter (which AA already part-owns & operates).  South Africa - Hillside smelter:  immediate cash flow accretive; same AP30 smelting technology that already operate; power contract through 2031.
*	Deal size is $4.7-5.4bn=$3.1 bn cash + $1 bn AA stock (17 mln shares) + $600m of lease-related debt + $750m of contingent payments linked to metals prices (see Fig 3).  S32 is flagging $1.2bn of rehabilitation liabilities in its presentation but AA noted that these are ~$400m under US GAAP on their call.
*	Approvals could take up to 12 months and close in 1H27.  S32 will have a shareholder vote in Oct/Nov - early signs are positive with the stock trading up in Australia.
*	No intention to raise additional equity given expected strong FCF generation in 2H, plus asset sales, plus Ma'aden shares.  

	
	

Figure 1. Alcoa Pro-Forma

Read Full Report <https://www.citivelocity.> 	
 	Alexander Hacking, CFAAC <https://www.citivelocity.com/directory/eppublic/analyst/#/7050/overview>  
alex.hacking@citi.com <mailto:alex.hacking@citi.com?subject=RE:Sizing%20Up%20With%20S32%20Assets%3B%20Accretive%20On%20Citi's%20Bullish%20Price%20Deck.>  
+1-212-816-6232 <tel:+1-212-816-6232>  
	
Alcoa Corp
Valuation
Earnings and earnings-based valuations of Alcoa are highly sensitive to alumina/aluminum price assumptions. Our target price of $76/sh is based on applying an ~8x multiple to our mid-cycle EBITDA estimate of US$2.5bn. This is an above-average multiple, but Citi has a rather bullish medium-term outlook for aluminum.  
Risks
Risks include the following: 

вҖў Aluminum and Alumina Prices вҖ“ The markets Alcoa competes in are highly cyclical and capital-intensive. If the prices of alumina or aluminum were to fall, Alcoa would see a significant impact to earnings. 

вҖў China вҖ“ The global aluminum market remains oversupplied by China and highly dependent on Chinese government policy. 

вҖў Raw Materials вҖ“ Rising/lowered costs of raw materials (including energy) could negatively/positively impact Alcoa's profitability. 

вҖў Political and Country Risk вҖ“ Alcoa is global, with operations in the US, Australia, Brazil, Canada, Europe, Guinea, and Saudi Arabia. 

If the impact on the company from any of these factors proves to be greater/less than we anticipate, we believe the stock will likely have difficulty achieving our target price or could outperform it. 
South32 Ltd
(S32.AX; A$3.9; 1; 30 Jun 26; 16:00)
Valuation
Our target price is a rounded A$6.1 based on DCF and multiples valuation. Our DCF valuation uses a 9.0% real, after-tax, unlevered discount rate and terminal growth rate of 2%. Our long-term equilibrium commodity prices and other key assumptions are based on our commodities teamвҖҷs latest forecasts. Our multiples analysis focuses on 6.0x EV/EBITDA multiples (in-line with peer average). We derive our 12-month target price from a 50:50 weighting of div adj. NPV and earnings multiples analysis.
Risks
We see the following key risks to the achievement of our target price: 

South32вҖҷs earnings are linked to prices in world commodity markets, which have been subject to substantial volatility recently. These prices are in turn influenced by economic, new government regulations and geopolitical factors, notably the Chinese economy. A slowdown in Chinese economic growth would therefore adversely impact prices and consequently South32вҖҷs revenue. South32вҖҷs diversification across aluminium, alumina, manganese, nickel, silver and coal has the potential to mitigate this risk.  

The majority of South32вҖҷs revenues are in US dollars but with mines and processing plants in a number of countries, the influence of other currencies such as the A$, Brazilian real, South African rand on the operating cost base is equally significant. Fluctuations in these currencies can therefore heavily impact South32вҖҷs earnings. 

If the impact on the company from any of these factors proves to be greater than we anticipate, the stock will likely have difficulty achieving our target price. However, should they be less than anticipated, the stock could trade above our target price. 
South32 Ltd
(S32.L; ВЈ2.04; 1; 30 Jun 26; 16:30)
Valuation
Our target price is a rounded ВЈ3.2 based on DCF and multiples valuation. Our DCF valuation uses a 9.0% real, after-tax, unlevered discount rate and terminal growth rate of 2%. Our long-term equilibrium commodity prices and other key assumptions are based on our commodities teamвҖҷs latest forecasts. Our multiples analysis focuses on 6.0x EV/EBITDA multiples (in-line with peer average). We derive our 12-month target price from a 50:50 weighting of div adj. NPV and earnings multiples analysis.
Risks
We see the following key risks to the achievement of our target price: 
 South32вҖҷs earnings are linked to prices in world commodity markets, which have been subject to substantial volatility recently. These prices are in turn influenced by economic, new government regulations and geopolitical factors, notably the Chinese economy. A slowdown in Chinese economic growth would therefore adversely impact prices and consequently South32вҖҷs revenue. South32вҖҷs diversification across aluminium, alumina, manganese, nickel, silver and coal has the potential to mitigate this risk.    
The majority of South32вҖҷs revenues are in US dollars but with mines and processing plants in a number of countries, the influence of other currencies such as the A$, Brazilian real, South African rand on the operating cost base is equally significant. Fluctuations in these currencies can therefore heavily impact South32вҖҷs earnings.   
If the impact on the company from any of these factors proves to be greater than we anticipate, the stock will likely have difficulty achieving our target price. However, should they be less than anticipated, the stock could trade above our target price.

emails.subject: GLASS/PANELS - the AI story - TSM vs INTC,  GLW,  Innolux vs BOE,  ABF Substrates
emails.body:
SALES COMMENTARY ONLY (NOT A PRODUCT OF RESEARCH)

For institutional use onLY

 

I used to say silicon wafers were the final frontier of AI penetrating the deepest parts of the supply chain. But I stand corrected вҖ“ Panel stocks would be one of the last and most surprising areas of tech to finally see AI benefit the numbers. ItвҖҷs still early to see any financial impact, but with Innolux +300% YTD and GLWвҖҷs recent rally, IвҖҷm getting more questions whatвҖҷs going on in glass/panels. IвҖҷll do my best to summarize whatвҖҷs going on, but for the in-the-weeds technology discussion, our Greater China Hardware analyst Derrick Yang is a great resource <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/295f25ba-6472-11f1-918b-4841bb500f53?t=1782857414:7515:42924:816774ccf5ea&m=1&ch=Email Safety Catch#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==> , along with of course Head of Greater China Semis Charlie Chan and US Networking Equipment analyst Meta Marshall (her take on GLW here <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/ef10c80c-73d8-11f1-9560-223b52d1e427?t=1782857414:7515:42924:816774ccf5ea&m=1&ch=Email Safety Catch#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==> )

 

 

The source of this excitement on glass/panels:  TSMC vs INTC.  Specifically in Advanced Packaging.  ItвҖҷs well telegraphed at this point that the upcoming version of INTCвҖҷs advanced packaging technology (EMIB-T) has performance and cost advantages over that of TSMC (CoWoS). These advantages theoretically are evident when we get to very large chips (i.e. above 9.7 reticle-size). To be fair, excitement on EMIB-T is still early. The original EMIB failed for multiple reasons, but this time market is more excited for EMIB-T due to 1) much higher sample yields (although Howard Kao notes Substrate makers worry production yields will still be low); and 2) the warpage and cost advantages vs CoWoS in these large chips. Therefore, if we assume EMIB-T can be a viable technology, the market believes TSMC will more aggressively adopt glass/panel across parts of its advanced packaging technology to compete.

 

 

Before I go further, IвҖҷll emphasize again that CoWoS vs EMIB-T is the key driver of painting any blue sky. If market sours on EMIB-T, the 2nd derivative impact may be thinking TSMC needs to less aggressively adopt parts of below technologies. And IвҖҷll emphasize that from a stock perspective, itвҖҷs ok to NOT be too early on these potentially long-dated technology shifts. Derrick and team wrote a really great deep-drive in 2024 <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/a7bf4330-91f7-11ef-9743-347dd77fb5d0?t=1782857414:7515:42924:816774ccf5ea&m=1&ch=Email Safety Catch#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==> , but their message then was itвҖҷs too early. Just comparing Nittobo vs Innolux (the former being a perceived loser from вҖңGlass Core SubstrateвҖқ), sure Innolux has outperformed YTD, but Nittobo is up 1055% since Jan 2023 vs Innolux 408%.

 

 

With this context, I will get into the 2 technologies that TSMC is potentially using to close the gap vs EMIB-T:

 

1) Glass Core Substrate

 

The goal is to solve for warpage in CoWoS as we get to larger chips.

 

In вҖңCoWoSвҖқ, this changes the вҖңoSвҖқ aka Substrate. Right now, the Substrate is made using an organic CCL (think Resonac/Mitsubishi Gas, who require T-glass from Nittobo). The ABF layers goes on top of the CCL. But in Glass Core, it replaces the organic CCL with a Glass Core. ItвҖҷs essentially a piece of glass that uses вҖңTGVвҖқ for vertical connectivity (TGV = Though Glass Vias, aka holes, think like TSV when we talk about HBM). After the Glass layer, then there is copper layer, then ABF, then copper, then ABF, etc.

 

So if we just take this on face value, market assumes itвҖҷs a negative for CCL and T-glass, but still a positive for ABF Substrate (Ibiden, etc) along with Glass supplier (e.g. GLW) and Panel makers (e.g. Innolux). The panel maker is primarily responsible for drilling the TGV holes, and the glass maker would need to come up with the right chemistry for the glass, both working to make cracks in the glass much less likely.

 

Timing wise, Derrick says adoption is more likely 2H28, vs market assumes CoPoS comes in 2029. That means theoretically speaking, Glass Core Substrate wouldnвҖҷt contribute to full year earnings until 2029 (the mass production year for Feiynman).

 

What got market excited was that on June 11th, TSMC at an event in Japan talked about Glass Core Substrate and specifically listed Ibiden and Innolux on a slide as development partners. Derrick checks suggest what this TSMC slide means is just that proof of concept is completed. I.e. TSMC has validated the benefits over organic. But now is when design starts for the product and spec. Then gets tested again. And after this, TSMC might release the spec to the industry and look for more partners.

 

Derrick says theoretically speaking, if Innolux and Ibiden are development partners with TSMC, they would like have high share in the first generation at mass production. But he would assume TSMC would want more supply chain partners from resiliency perspective. 

 

On the Panel side, Derrick says AUO is not focused on this, so the competitive threat is BOE (he is OW). But BOEвҖҷs strategy is different. BOE wants to do the entire Glass Core Substrate stack, so both Glass Core and the Substrate (vs splitting Innolux / Ibiden). IвҖҷm personally a little surprised and wonder where BOE would have built the ABF Substrate know-how from (remember that Unimicron still struggles to gain meaningful share vs Ibiden within NVDA). But BOE does have global aspiration ex NVDA, so not just supporting ChinaвҖҷs ecosystem. Derrick says BOEвҖҷs strategy is to work with other GPU and ASIC makers who may need to transition to Glass Core / TGV, and if they build partnerships with the fabless customers, that may cause the fabless customers to push TSMC to use BOE as a supplier.

 

Financial impact вҖ“ IвҖҷll avoid speculating any specifics in writing. Again Derrick says the full year contribution wouldnвҖҷt be until ~2029, assuming the timeline is on-track. This is part of why heвҖҷs still EW on Innolux. However, he thinks Innolux could capture a large part of the value for a 14 reticle-size substrate. So doing ((ASP * # of NVDA GPUs in 2029) * InnoluxвҖҷs value capture), that implies a potentially large contribution relative to InnoluxвҖҷs total 2025 revenue. 

 

Derrick just introduced his 2028 Innolux estimates, where he assumes NTD 20bn in Semi-related revenue (vs Total 2028e Rev of  NTD 316bn).

 

 

2) CoPoS

 

The goal is to solve for yield (i.e. cost).

 

CoPoS replaces the вҖңoWвҖқ with a Panel. Essentially the problem with CoWoS is that you are cutting square chips out of a circular wafer. As the chips are bigger, you get less chips from each wafer, and more of the edges of that circle get wasted (see picture below). That causes lower yield / higher cost for the customer. But given a Panel is square, it can be cut into squares and fully utilized.

 

In terms of the winners, it depends what the Panel is used for. If the Panel is used as a вҖңcarrierвҖқ, from what Derrick sees today, itвҖҷs mainly TSMC and subsidiaries working on it / potentially capturing the value. But if the Panel is used as an interposer, that creates more opportunity for Panel makers. ThatвҖҷs because the glass interposer would likely need TGV (the holes), and even more so in the same surface area. Derrick assumes timing wise, if this all happens, the panel would first be used as a carrier, and then later on as an interposer.

 

To be fair, TSMC is also exploring versions of CoWoS that try to be similar to EMIB-T and would be an alternative to going forward with CoPoS. But from what the team sees now, the different iterations have drawbacks. Such as sacrificing chip performance (hard to imagine NVDA liking that) or relying on Substrate companies for critical processes (INTC is seemingly ok to do that, but TSMC historically prefers to do critical processes in-house). Therefore, Charlie thinks itвҖҷs more likely TSMC pulls forward CoPoS from 2029 to 2028 to compete with EMIB-T, rather than trying to create an EMIB-T-like product.

 



 

 

Equipment / Materials

 

I want to better understand the supply chain myself before coming to conclusions. Please see toward the end of the deep-dive from 202 <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/a7bf4330-91f7-11ef-9743-347dd77fb5d0?t=1782857414:7515:42924:816774ccf5ea&m=1&ch=Email Safety Catch#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==> 4 with lots of great single-stock commentary from Derrick & the Japan Semi Cap & Materials teams. MS Greater China Semis analyst Tiffany Yeh has also since noted that Allring would still be a beneficiary despite bulk of revenue today coming from CoWoS. She expects <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/6276603e-341b-11f1-a3a8-4c6a3d6dc478?t=1782857414:7515:42924:816774ccf5ea&m=1&ch=Email Safety Catch#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==>  the CoP TAM to be at least one-third of the current "oS" process, adding NT$50вҖ“70mn revenue per kwpm of expansion.

 

In his note, Derrick says the challenges lie in forming TGVs with laser-induced etching and the subsequent copper plating, because this is not part of the regular display panel production to which Innolux's expertise is related. His understanding is that Innolux will need to procure new equipment for glass cores and the initial capex might be NT$20-30bn.

 

Something also to note is that itвҖҷs extremely unlikely Glass Core or CoPoS could вҖңtightenвҖқ the overal Panel market. Innolux has to build separate capacity to support Glass Core. This is important because what really made Front-End SPE work is when AI became big enough (i.e. spread beyond HBM and GPUs) to tighten the broader logic and memory markets. ThatвҖҷs when Foundry and Memory customers started to aggressively expand spend from Back-end SPE to Front-end SPE. Therefore, for now. I personally think the focus should be more вҖңdownstreamвҖқ (glass, panel, ABF substrate makers) to capture the value, but IвҖҷm early days on this.

 

 

 

Happy to discuss more and connect you with Derrick, Charlie, Meta, Shawn Kim (LGD, Samsung), Sato san (Ibiden), Howard Kao (Unimicron), Tiffany, Watanabe san (Resonac, MGC), Hasegawa san (Nittobo), and Tamura san (Japan SPE).

 

 

Reports:

Greater China вҖ“ Displays: Progress for Glass in Advanced Packaging among Panel Makers (29 Jun 2026) <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/295f25ba-6472-11f1-918b-4841bb500f53?t=1782857414:7515:42924:816774ccf5ea&m=1&ch=Email Safety Catch#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==> 

TSMC: Domination in leading-edge foundry continues; OW (29 Jun 2026) <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/f213bb56-7354-11f1-9560-223b52d1e427?t=1782857414:7515:42924:816774ccf5ea&m=1&ch=Email Safety Catch#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==>
