emails.subject: TSEM Susq_30 jun 26 (30/06)
emails.to: Daniel Grozdea; Felipe Monteiro; Fernanda Watkins
emails.body:
Tower Semiconductor: Optical Interconnect Technology & Market Update


Insights from a call with Marco and Amol from Tower Semiconductor, discussing the evolving landscape of optical transceivers, silicon photonics, and AI infrastructure interconnects.


1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY


*	The optical interconnect market is experiencing significant, multi-year growth, driven by accelerating data rates (currently **1.6T**, with **3.2T** next) and expanding AI/datacenter build-outs. This includes both exploding unit volumes and technology migration.
*	A critical transition is underway from traditional pluggable transceivers to **Near Package Optics (NPO)**, with NPO expected to see significant production volume in 2025 (some in 2024), particularly for **1.6T** deployments. NPO, by bringing optics closer to the ASIC, offers enhanced performance and content opportunities.
*	Tower Semiconductor (TSEM) is a direct beneficiary, seeing higher content per module in NPO due to potential for more integrated lanes and combined transmit/receive (Tx/Rx) functions on its silicon photonics (SiPho) dies.
*	TSEM's **heterogeneous integration platform**, which embeds Indium Phosphide (InP) lasers directly onto SiPho wafers, is a key immediate opportunity. This technology, already in early production, simplifies module assembly, improves reliability, and expands TSEM's serviceable available market (SAM) to include high-value laser components.
*	The widespread adoption of **Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)** is seen as a longer-term event, potentially diminished and delayed by the success and sufficiency of NPO in addressing many current challenges like signal loss. CPO presents significant integration, thermal, and testing hurdles.
*	TSEMвҖҷs **200mm SiPho platforms** remain highly competitive and often more cost-effective than 300mm for current optical applications, though TSEM is strategically expanding its **300mm capacity** (pending a METI grant) for future larger form factor or specialized applications.
*	New players and startups (e.g., **Cintil**, **Salience**, **Oriel**, some backed by **Nvidia**) are leveraging TSEM's advanced foundry capabilities to drive innovation in integrated lasers and optical switching. Overall tone is bullish on TSEM's positioning and the broader optical interconnect market.

________________________________


3. KEY TAKEAWAYS BY THEME


Optical Interconnect Market Growth & Data Rate Migration BULL


The speaker highlighted an explosive increase in the number of units and a rapid progression in data rates. The market has moved from **400 Gigabit per second (Gbps)** a few years ago, through **800 Gbps** last year, and is now transitioning to **1.6 Terabit per second (Tbps)** as the dominant data rate, with **3.2T** expected next. This migration necessitates technology refreshes in both silicon photonics (SiPho) and silicon germanium (SiGe) components, as they must double their operating speed. This leads to more value for the customer and for Tower Semiconductor.

*	Why it matters: Sustained demand for higher-speed optical interconnects directly correlates with the growth in AI infrastructure and hyperscale data centers. This trend drives unit growth and increases the complexity and value of individual components, benefiting specialized foundries like Tower Semiconductor.
*	Investment read-through: This dynamic ensures a multi-year growth runway for the optical interconnect sector and its enablers.


Transition from Pluggable to Near Package Optics (NPO) and Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) NEUTRAL


The discussion provided a clear differentiation between pluggable, NPO, and CPO architectures:

*	Pluggable: 
*	Near Package Optics (NPO):higher content per die due to more integrated lanes and a trend towards combining transmit and receive functions.
*	Co-Packaged Optics (CPO): 

NPO is described as a "good solution" that solves most of the problems addressed by CPO without incurring all the complexities. It's closer to being "real" and already available. The speaker suggests that NPO, as it gains traction, has the potential to minimize the perceived benefit of CPO and push out its widespread adoption to some degree. NPO is expected to see significant production volumes next year (2025), with some activity in **2024**, aligning with **1.6T** deployments. It could also be sufficient to enable "scale up" within the rack.

*	Why it matters: 
*	Investment read-through:Positive for NPO adoption in the near to medium term, benefiting TSEM with higher content. BULL

Both SiPho and SiGe technologies are crucial for optical transceivers. SiPho primarily handles the transmit modulator side, while SiGe is used for drivers and Transimpedance Amplifiers (TIAs) on the receive and transmit electronic pieces. Both technologies are seeing significant growth, with SiPho experiencing **3-4x year-over-year growth** and SiGe **20-30% year-over-year growth** in recent years.

The migration to NPO further enhances opportunities for both: 

	*	SiPho: 
	*	SiGe: 
	*	Why it matters: 
	*	Investment read-through:Strong positive for Tower Semiconductor, indicating sustained and growing demand for its core offerings in the optical space.


Heterogeneous Integration of Indium Phosphide Lasers on SiPho BULL


Tower Semiconductor has announced and is in early production of a platform that integrates Indium Phosphide (InP) lasers directly onto silicon photonic wafers. This is a significant development:

	*	Technology: 
	*	Benefits for Customers: 
	*	Benefits for Tower:Bundles two components into one, significantly expanding Tower's serviceable available market (SAM) to include the high-value laser component. It also offers enhanced reliability (hermetically sealed within the wafer, no 3-5 material exposed to moisture) and can help with thermal management by enabling external laser sources for CPO or NPO applications.
	*	Market Application:pluggable and NPO markets and does not require CPO to become widespread.
	*	Why it matters: 
	*	Investment read-through:Highly positive for TSEM, indicating a clear path to increased revenue per optical component and a strengthened competitive position.


Optical Switching (OCS) & New Applications BULL


Beyond traditional optical transceivers, the embedded InP/heterogeneous integration structure is also being used for optical switching on-die, acting as an amplifier or attenuator for optical signals. This is being driven by new companies like **Salience** and **Oriel**, which are bringing these switches to market.

Other potential applications mentioned include:

	*	**Autonomous Driving:** LiDAR, specifically FMCW LiDAR, which provides more data (velocity of each point).
	*	**Quantum Computing / Optical Computing:** Leveraging the base SiPho technology.
	*	**Satellite Communication:** Not optical, but high-frequency SiGe for beam steering/forming in terrestrial units for satellite internet.
	*	Why it matters: 
	*	Investment read-through:Positive for long-term growth and market diversification, especially as new AI architectures emerge.


Wafer Size & Process Technology (200mm vs. 300mm) NEUTRAL


The speaker addressed the perceived advantage of 300mm wafers for SiPho, particularly by larger foundries. Tower's stance is nuanced:

	*	**Current Advantage of 200mm:** For current SiPho applications up to **1.6T**, 200mm is often a better and more cost-effective platform. This is because the line width requirements are adequately met by 200mm, and certain optical functions benefit from aluminum interconnects (common in 200mm) over copper (common in 300mm). Moreover, much of the light is carried in nitride layers, where 200mm can perform better.
	*	**300mm Future:** While 200mm currently dominates, Tower recognizes some future products (larger form factors, certain receive functions) may benefit from 300mm. The company has internal 300mm technology and is planning a significant expansion of 300mm capacity in its Japan factory, pending a **METI grant approval**.
	*	Why it matters: 
	*	Investment read-through:Neutral from a competitive standpoint, as Tower appears well-positioned across both wafer sizes to meet market demands. Capacity expansion in 300mm could be a future positive.


Higher Speed Modulators (400Gbps per lane) BULL


Tower is actively working on technologies to double the speed per lane from **200Gbps** to **400Gbps**. Multiple approaches are being pursued:

	*	Silicon-based: 
	*	Indium Phosphide (InP) based: 
	*	New materials: 
	*	Why it matters: 
	*	Investment read-through:Positive, indicating a sustained leadership in technology development for advanced optical components.

________________________________


4. COMPANY-BY-COMPANY READ-THROUGH


Tower Semiconductor (TSEM) BULL


Summary: Tower is positioned as a direct and significant beneficiary of all major trends discussed. Its SiPho and SiGe platforms are critical for next-gen optical transceivers. The ramp of NPO is expected to increase its content per module. Its new heterogeneous integration platform for InP lasers is already in early production, expanding its SAM to high-value laser components and offering strong differentiation. The company is actively pursuing next-generation technologies like 400Gbps/lane modulators and expanding 300mm capacity.

Positive Implications: Increased revenue from higher unit volumes and greater content per module. Enhanced gross margins due to increased value capture from integrated laser components. Strong competitive position as an open-ecosystem foundry partner for a wide range of optical innovators. Future growth avenues in optical switching and beyond data centers.

Financial Impact: Likely positive impact on future revenue and potentially gross margins as higher-value, more complex SiPho dies with integrated lasers gain traction. Continued strong growth in SiPho and SiGe translates to core business expansion.

Investor takeaway: Direct Beneficiary. TSEM is a crucial enabler for the AI infrastructure build-out through its specialized optical foundry services and innovative platforms. This positions it for sustained growth.


Nvidia (NVDA) BULL


Summary: While not a direct speaker, Nvidia is mentioned as backing startups like **Cintil** and **Salience** that are leveraging Tower's advanced optical platforms. This highlights Nvidia's strategic interest and investment in next-generation optical interconnects for its AI compute clusters.

Positive Implications: Nvidia's involvement underscores the importance of these advanced optical technologies for future AI infrastructure. Their backing of these startups, which use Tower's platforms, validates the direction of innovation and the necessity of improved interconnects for **NVDA's** ecosystems.

Investor takeaway: Indirect Beneficiary. The transcript reinforces that advanced optical interconnects are critical for **NVDA's** AI roadmap, and **NVDA** is actively investing in the ecosystem, even at the startup level, which flows back to key foundry partners like **TSEM**.


Coherent (COHR) BULL


Summary: Coherent was mentioned as a partner that demonstrated a **400Gbps** link using Tower's silicon photonic technology at OFC.

Positive Implications: Collaboration with Tower on next-generation high-speed links. Shows **COHR** is actively participating in the evolution of optical technology.

Investor takeaway: Direct Beneficiary. A key player in the optical component ecosystem, benefiting from continued technological advancements and demand for higher data rates.


OpenLight BULL


Summary: OpenLight partnered with Tower to demonstrate a **400Gbps** link using the heterogeneously integrated Indium Phosphide technology at OFC.

Positive Implications: Demonstrates the versatility of Tower's heterogeneous integration platform beyond just lasers, extending to high-speed modulators. **OpenLight** is at the forefront of utilizing these advanced capabilities.

Investor takeaway: Direct Beneficiary. A design partner benefiting from advanced foundry capabilities for next-gen optical components.


IQE (IQE.L) BULL


Summary: IQE is identified as Tower's partner supplying Indium Phosphide (InP) substrates plus Epi layers for the heterogeneous integration platform.

Positive Implications: Secures IQE's position as a critical supplier for a rapidly growing and high-value segment of advanced optical components. As Tower ramps its heterogeneous integration, IQE's business should grow.

Financial Impact: Increased demand for InP epi-wafers, potentially boosting IQE's revenue and market share in this specialized material segment.

Investor takeaway: Direct Beneficiary. A key enabler in the supply chain for advanced integrated optics.


Cintil (Private) BULL


Summary: Mentioned as a promising startup, backed by **Nvidia**, that is a customer for Tower's heterogeneous integration platform, specifically for the integrated laser source.

Positive Implications: Represents an innovative customer leveraging Tower's advanced technologies, potentially bringing disruptive products to market. Nvidia's backing adds credibility.

Investor takeaway: Indirect Beneficiary (for investors in **NVDA** through its ecosystem; direct beneficiary as a customer of **TSEM**). Represents future demand for **TSEM's** capabilities.


Salience (Private) BULL


Summary: Identified as a startup, also backed by **Nvidia**, utilizing Tower's platform for optical switching applications.

Positive Implications: Similar to Cintil, this highlights another innovative use case for Tower's technology, particularly in the emerging Optical Circuit Switching (OCS) market.

Investor takeaway: Indirect Beneficiary (for investors in **NVDA**; direct beneficiary as a customer of **TSEM**). Shows expansion into new market segments for **TSEM**.


Oriel (Private) BULL


Summary: Another new company mentioned as a customer for Tower's OCS solutions.

Positive Implications: Further evidence of new entrants driving innovation in optical switching using Tower's platforms.

Investor takeaway: Direct Beneficiary (as a customer of **TSEM**). Contributes to **TSEM's** diversification of customer base and applications.


TSMC (TSM) NEUTRAL


Summary: Mentioned as having a "Co-Op" platform (likely referring to **CoWoS** or similar co-packaging). The question was if Tower could be a partner or is a competitor.

Implications: Tower views its role as potentially supplying a SiPho or electrical IC die into such a packaging system. This indicates Tower does not see **TSMC** as a direct foundry competitor in specialized SiPho manufacturing but rather as a potential downstream partner for advanced packaging.

Investor takeaway: Unclear if any direct relationship exists today, but conceptually Tower could be a component supplier. No direct competitive threat in SiPho foundry is implied.


Corning (GLW) NEUTRAL


Summary: Mentioned as actively investing in standardized manufacturing of fiber array units (FAU), which is critical for attaching many fibers in reduced footprints.

Positive Implications: Solving the fiber attachment problem would facilitate broader and faster adoption of optical interconnects (NPO, CPO), which is indirectly positive for Tower and the entire optical ecosystem.

Investor takeaway: Indirect Beneficiary. Their efforts could remove a bottleneck for optical adoption, benefiting all players in the value chain.


GlobalFoundries (GFS) / Samsung (005930.KS) NEUTRAL


Summary: Referenced implicitly as competitors who have 300mm capabilities and tout an advantage with advanced CMOS nodes (e.g., 12nm, 28nm) for SiPho.

Implications: Tower's speaker argues that their 200mm SiPho processes are currently more suitable and cost-effective for optical functions, and their internal 300mm capabilities show no inherent advantage for most current SiPho products. This suggests that the general-purpose advanced CMOS nodes of **GFS** or **Samsung** do not automatically confer a SiPho advantage.

Investor takeaway: Unclear/Neutral. Tower implies its specialized foundry expertise in SiPho is more important than general CMOS process node leadership or wafer size for current applications. This challenges the narrative that larger, general-purpose foundries automatically win in SiPho.

________________________________


5. IMPORTANT NUMBERS / ASSUMPTIONS


	*	Silicon Photonics Growth: "Multiples every year, factors of three or four every year" in the last few years. (Headline: Very strong growth)
	*	Silicon Germanium Growth: "20 to 30% a year in the last couple of years." (Headline: Strong, consistent growth)
	*	Current Data Rate: **1.6 Terabit per second (Tbps)** is "taking over the 800 gig market" this year. (Headline: Rapid transition)
	*	Next Data Rate: **3.2 Tbps** is "looking at next." (Headline: Continued acceleration)
	*	Current Lane Speed: **200 Gigabit per second (Gbps) per lane** today.
	*	Future Lane Speed: Path to **400 Gbps per lane**. (Headline: Doubling speed per lane)
	*	NPO Adoption Timeline: "Next year (2025), we would see significant production volume," with "some also happening this year (2024)." (Headline: NPO commercialization ramping now, significant in 2025)
	*	Wafer Sizes: **200mm** vs. **300mm**. (Headline: Process node/wafer size not primary differentiator for SiPho today)
	*	METI Grant: Significant expansion of Tower's **300mm capacity** in its Japan factory is "pending the METI approval." (Assumption: Grant approval is key for 300mm expansion)

________________________________


6. BULL / BEAR / DEBATE


Bull Case Implications BULL


	*	Accelerated Optical Demand: The insatiable demand for AI compute drives an equally insatiable demand for high-speed, low-loss optical interconnects. The market is growing "multiples" annually for SiPho, indicating a secular boom.
	*	Tower's Strategic Position: Tower is an indispensable enabler, providing the foundational SiPho and SiGe technologies. Its open-ecosystem foundry approach allows it to capture growth from numerous innovative startups and established players.
	*	Increased Content & Value Capture: The transition to NPO increases Tower's content per module. More significantly, heterogeneous integration of lasers expands its SAM into high-value components, enhancing revenue and margin potential.
	*	Technological Leadership: Tower is actively investing in and demonstrating next-generation technologies (e.g., 400Gbps per lane, OCS), ensuring its relevance for future data rate and architectural shifts.


Bear Case Implications BEAR


	*	CPO Challenges & Delays: While NPO provides a buffer, the eventual transition to CPO presents significant manufacturing, testing, thermal, and yield hurdles. If these prove insurmountable or lead to unexpected architectural shifts, it could disrupt the current supply chain.
	*	Competition from Large Foundries: While currently not a major threat for SiPho, larger foundries with 300mm advanced CMOS capabilities might eventually make inroads, potentially pressuring pricing or market share in the long run.
	*	Niche Market Risk for New Applications: While OCS, LiDAR, and quantum computing offer diversification, their commercialization timelines and ultimate market size remain uncertain, potentially limiting near-term impact.


Key Debates or Uncertainties


	*	Pacing of NPO vs. CPO Adoption: Will NPO truly be "good enough" to significantly delay CPO, or will CPO's advantages for extreme scale-out eventually overcome its challenges faster than expected?
	*	Winning Scale-Up Technology: Within the rack, what specific technology (NPO, slow and wide, etc.) will emerge as the dominant solution for "scale-up" interconnects?
	*	Impact of 300mm for SiPho: When and for which applications will 300mm genuinely offer a compelling advantage over 200mm for SiPho, and how will Tower's capacity expansion timing align with this?


What needs to be monitored next


	*	The actual ramp and production volumes of NPO in **2024** and **2025**.
	*	Progress and commercial traction of Tower's heterogeneous integration platform, especially with its initial customers.
	*	Approval and timeline for Tower's **METI grant** and subsequent **300mm capacity expansion** in Japan.
	*	Further public announcements or demonstrations of 400Gbps per lane solutions and other next-generation optical technologies.
	*	Any signs of accelerated CPO adoption or breakthroughs from competitors that might change the NPO/CPO dynamic.

________________________________


7. MARKET / STOCK IMPLICATIONS


The transcript offers a generally positive read-through for stocks exposed to advanced optical interconnects and AI infrastructure, with a particularly strong positive implication for **Tower Semiconductor (TSEM)**.

	*	Positive Reacting Stocks/Sectors: 

		*	Tower Semiconductor (TSEM): Directly benefits from increased content, new high-value platforms (heterogeneous integration), and sustained demand. Reinforces its specialist foundry advantage.
		*	Optical Component & Module Manufacturers (e.g., LITE, COHR, possibly AVGO, MRVL): Continued strong demand for transceivers, data rate upgrades, and the NPO transition provide tailwinds. **COHR** is explicitly mentioned as a partner.
		*	Nvidia (NVDA): As the primary driver of AI compute demand, **NVDA** benefits from the advancements in interconnects that enable larger and more efficient clusters. Their backing of innovative optical startups further reinforces this.
		*	IQE (IQE.L): As a key supplier of InP epi-wafers for heterogeneous integration, stands to gain from Tower's success in this area.

	*	Narratives Reinforced: 

		*	The AI boom is a multi-year secular growth driver for semiconductor and optical infrastructure.
		*	Advanced packaging and integration (like NPO and heterogeneous integration) are critical for overcoming performance bottlenecks in AI systems.
		*	Specialized foundries like **TSEM**, with deep domain expertise in materials (SiPho, SiGe, InP), are vital enablers for innovation, even as general-purpose CMOS foundries focus on pure logic nodes.

	*	Changes to Estimates/Sentiment/Positioning: 

		*	The immediate ramp of NPO and the early production of heterogeneous integration could lead to upward revisions for **TSEM's** revenue and gross margin estimates, particularly for 2025 and beyond, as these higher-value offerings scale.
		*	The sentiment around the sustainability of optical growth is reinforced as positive, suggesting continued strong valuation support for leading players.
		*	Positioning towards companies with demonstrated leadership in advanced integration (like **TSEM**) and those well-aligned with the NPO transition is likely to be strengthened.

	*	Impact Horizon: The impact is both near-term (heterogeneous integration, NPO ramp in 2024/2025) and medium-to-long term (sustained data rate upgrades, future CPO, OCS, other applications).

________________________________


8. QUOTES / SPEAKER ATTRIBUTION


	*	Marco (Speaker 2): "Part of the growth you're seeing is of course the number of units are exploding. Part of it is technology continues to migrate forward on the on the data rate scale."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2): "So looking forward, there's more opportunity to replace copper in scale up so within the rack. And we can talk about that. There's more opportunity to build more clusters, so kind of scale across opportunities for optical interconnects."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on NPO vs. Pluggable: "The difference is really just in in how and where within the board the electrical to optical transition occurs."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on NPO content: "Theoretically, going from pluggable to NPO, should help with a higher content for tower... it'll be a little bit higher content because you may have a higher probability of integrating the receive. And and a higher level of technology because now in many cases you have higher number of lanes integrated in a single die, which is more difficult to do."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on CPO disruption: "It'll cause some disruption in the growth of the transceivers because now some of that growth will be absorbed in in CPO. Right. We don't see pluggables going away. We don't see NPO going away anytime soon."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on NPO potentially delaying CPO: "So we see our our view, my view is is that NPO as it as it gains traction, as it's starting to, has the potential to minimize the benefit of CPO and push that out to some to some degree."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on heterogeneous integration application: "For us, that's a huge opportunity. That's basically now taking the laser Sam and Tam and now bringing it or the Tam and bringing it into our Sam, or our serviceable market."
	*	Amol (Speaker 3) on heterogeneous integration benefits: "Because of the NPO architecture, you're not really putting it on A6. So you're also solving the thermal problem. So it solves your cost problem. It solves your laser problem, uh laser power problem. It also reduces the number of components."
	*	Amol (Speaker 3) on heterogeneous integration quality: "Where, by the way, there is no 3.5 material exposed to moisture or environment. Right. So by definition, it actually gives you much more reliable laser."
	*	Amol (Speaker 3) on partner ecosystem: "Cintil is backed by Nvidia... Salience is backed also. So there are lots of places where the end customer is still Nvidia driving."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on 200mm vs. 300mm for SiPho: "Line width that we can achieve in 200 mm are perfectly adequate for this type of application... 200 mm is a has been a better platform. Both more cost effective."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on NPO adoption: "Our expectation is next year, we would see significant production volume. So it's hard to say exactly where we are today. We're running some. Is it early production? Is it qualification runs? But but certainly next year we see significant adoption of NPO."
	*	Marco (Speaker 2) on NPO/Pluggable vs CPO evolution: "It is very likely that a NPO or pluggable form factor will allow you to get to 400 gigabit per second faster than you could do it in a CPO."

________________________________


9. BOTTOM LINE


Here are the 3вҖ“6 most important conclusions for an investor:

	66.	The AI-driven optical interconnect market is experiencing **explosive growth** in units and data rates (now at **1.6T**, moving to **3.2T**), providing a strong, multi-year tailwind for specialized foundries like **Tower Semiconductor**.
	67.	The transition to **Near Package Optics (NPO)** is ramping significantly in **2025** (with early deployments in **2024**), offering increased content and value capture for **TSEM** per module due to greater integration (more lanes, combined Tx/Rx).
	68.	**TSEM's** proprietary **heterogeneous integration of InP lasers on SiPho** is a near-term, high-value differentiator, expanding its SAM to include critical laser components and delivering significant supply chain simplification and reliability benefits to customers.
	69.	The widespread commercialization of **Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)** is likely to be delayed by NPO's effectiveness and CPO's inherent technical complexities, providing a longer runway for **TSEM's** current product offerings.
	70.	**TSEM's** strong technical expertise in SiPho and SiGe, combined with a flexible 200mm/300mm strategy, positions it well against larger, more general-purpose foundry competitors, making it a critical strategic partner for innovators (including **NVDA-backed** startups).

________________________________


10. FOCUS-AREA READ-THROUGH


SemiCaps BULL


The transcript is positive. The discussion around **Tower Semiconductor's (TSEM)** specialized SiPho and SiGe foundry processes (both 200mm and planned 300mm expansion) highlights the critical role of custom process technology in enabling advanced optical components. The heterogeneous integration of InP lasers onto SiPho wafers represents a significant advancement in advanced-packaging tools and materials, specifically **hybrid bonding** (attaching InP to Si). This process, done entirely by TSEM, increases the complexity and value of the fabricated dies. The move to **1.6T** and **3.2T** data rates, and **400Gbps per lane**, drives demand for advanced process capabilities. This suggests continued strong demand for specialized **WFE/capex intensity** in optical-specific processes. What changes: Reinforces that not all foundry capacity is equal; specialized process technology for optics is a key differentiator. Monitor next: TSEM's 300mm expansion details (METI grant), adoption rates of heterogeneous integration.


Memory NEUTRAL


The transcript does not directly address DRAM/NAND/HBM metrics like ASPs, bit growth, or supply-demand dynamics. It touches on the *need* for higher speed interconnects due to compute (and by extension, memory traffic), but doesn't provide specific read-throughs for **MU**, **SK Hynix**, or **Samsung** beyond the general trend of AI driving infrastructure. What changes: Nothing specific for memory positioning. Monitor next: Indirectly, faster optical interconnects enable more powerful compute clusters that would consume more **HBM**. This is a standing bridge, not a direct read-through here.


800V Power NEUTRAL


The transcript is not addressed directly. There is no mention of 800VDC, power-per-rack, PSUs, VRMs, GaN/SiC power semis, or grid bottlenecks. What changes: Nothing. Monitor next: N/A.


Networking BULL


The transcript is positive. It directly addresses the evolution of the AI-DC fabric, specifically **optics** (transceivers, NPO, CPO, lasers, PICs/EICs). The shift from **800G** to **1.6T** and then **3.2T** highlights the rapid upgrade cycle. **Near Package Optics (NPO)** is explicitly called out as a significant near-term trend with ramp starting **2024** and major production in **2025**, potentially delaying **CPO**. **Tower (TSEM)** is a key supplier for the underlying silicon photonics (**SiPho**) and silicon germanium (**SiGe**) components, including next-gen **400Gbps per lane** modulators and heterogeneously integrated lasers. The mention of **Cintil**, **Salience**, and **Oriel** (some backed by **NVDA**) leveraging TSEM for integrated lasers and optical switching (**OCS**) points to innovation in **AI-DC fabric** beyond traditional pluggables. What changes: Reinforces the secular growth in optical interconnects driven by AI, with a clear roadmap for NPO adoption in the near term. Favors **TSEM** as a key enabler. Monitor next: The actual ramp of NPO, particularly for **AVGO** and **MRVL** who are significant players in connectivity silicon and overall merchant transceivers, and companies like **Coherent**.


CPUs NEUTRAL


The transcript is not addressed directly. There is no specific discussion of server & client CPUs, x86 vs. ARM, custom DC CPUs, or CPU allocation/pricing. The *need* for optical interconnects is to serve ASICs (presumably GPUs/AI accelerators), but no direct commentary on CPU trends. What changes: Nothing. Monitor next: N/A.


AI-Labs / IPOs & Capex BULL


The transcript is positive. The core premise for the growth in optical interconnects is the massive capex by **hyperscalers (MSFT, AMZN, GOOGL, META)** and the needs of frontier **AI labs (Anthropic, OpenAI, xAI)** for training and inference. The "exploding units" and rapid data rate increases are a direct result of these entities building out larger and more performant AI compute clusters. The mention of **Nvidia (NVDA)** backing startups like **Cintil** and **Salience** further links this to the demand for custom silicon and the compute bottleneck in AI. What changes: Reinforces the strong capex trends for AI infrastructure. Monitor next: Hyperscaler earnings calls for capex guidance and comments on optical deployment trends.


Cross-theme read-throughs


	*	HBM/hybrid-bonding/3D-DRAM вҶ’ Memory+SemiCaps: While not directly about HBM, the need for higher-speed interconnects (like NPO) implicitly supports the demand for advanced memory (HBM) and the advanced packaging (like CoWoS) required for these dense compute systems. The heterogeneous integration by TSEM involves a form of advanced packaging/bonding, which bridges SemiCaps and the eventual integration with logic/memory.
	*	CPO/transceiver-test/SerDes вҶ’ Networking+SemiCaps+CPUs: The discussion highlights the technical challenges of CPO, including testing. The transition to NPO means that SerDes capabilities must continue to evolve in proximity to the ASIC, benefiting **AVGO** and **MRVL**. TSEM's role as a foundry for SiPho/SiGe directly contributes to the networking layer.
	*	custom silicon/compute-bottleneck вҶ’ AI-Labs+Memory+Networking: The entire conversation stems from the compute-as-bottleneck problem in AI. Hyperscalers and AI labs require custom silicon (GPUs, ASICs) that are increasingly dependent on high-bandwidth, low-latency optical networking provided by companies like TSEM and its customers. This drives the need for advanced memory (HBM) and networking.
	*	power-per-rack вҶ’ 800V+Networking/CPUs: While 800V was not discussed, the NPO/CPO transition for higher data rates indirectly relates to power efficiency. Moving optics closer to the ASIC reduces electrical signal loss, which in turn reduces power consumption on the board, contributing to better power-per-rack metrics.
	*	CPU allocation & TSMC capacity вҶ’ CPUs+SemiCaps+Memory: No direct read-through, but the advanced process technology for optical components (SiPho) described by TSEM is complementary to the advanced logic/memory manufacturing done by **TSMC**.

Net focus-area impact: Networking and SemiCaps are the most actionable themes, driven by **Tower Semiconductor's** strong positioning in optical interconnects for AI infrastructure.

emails.subject: JPM US Market Intelligence | Afternoon Briefing: Stocks closed higher; NFP and ISM-Mfg previews: June 30, 2026
emails.body:
JUNE 30, 2026


 

IDEAS & INSIGHTS вҖ“ IN BRIEF

Market Intelligence will not be publishing July 2 вҖ“ July 6.

*       NFP SCENARIO ANALYSIS вҖ“ Feroli sees 125k jobs being added, versus the StreetвҖҷs estimate of 110k and 172k jobs in the prior print. For the unemployment rate (U.3) he sees 4.3% vs. 4.3% prior. For Average Hourly Earnings, he sees +0.2% MoM and +3.4% YoY.

*       US MKT INTEL VIEW вҖ“ we maintain our Tactical Bullish call and update the Monetization Menu

*       JPM RESEARCH MID-YEAR OUTLOOK вҖ“ Samantha Azzarello summarizes each outlook into 2-3 bullet points with relevant charts; her note is here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514903&ad=4537353171&h=HYabk8R&sig=OpHRtJIWCRgS8Bk6md3manbhvP0&v=2&isig=OqrRUUxiEiy7noowKSgoBsQX3UY&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-s2g3jjf/GPS-5334406-0/37539fe9-4bdf-465e-952c-42e15277b2a2> . Copies of each outlook are linked in her note.

 

AFTERNOON UPDATES (NEWS LINKS)

*       SPX +0.8%, NDX +1.7%, RTY +0.5%. WTI -98bps at $70.06, NatGas +236bps to $3.26, UK NatGas +173bps to ВЈ1.0433, Gold +2bps to $4,017, Silver +137bps to $59.06, 10Y @ 4.453%, and VIX @ 16.45.

*       US: Stocks closed higher despite narrow leadership. Tech again did the heavy-lifting on todayвҖҷs risk-on rally, while nearly 60% of the SPX actually finished lower today. Bond yields added 5-8bp across the curve; on the macro level, todayвҖҷs upside surprise in JOLTS definitely contributed to the overall bond selloff, but the anticipation ahead of NFP Thursday and amplified volatility during a shorten holiday week may also contribute to todayвҖҷs moves. Thematically, Seis, China Exposure, Cyclicals, Tariff Short and Momentum Winners are among the top performing baskets; Defensives lagged.

*       EU/UK: Major markets closed mostly higher led by SX5E and Germany. Thematically, Semi, AI and Renewables outperformed, while Software and Luxury lagged. UKX +0.1%, SX5E +1.5%, SXXP +0.9%, DAX +1.5%.

 

CATALYSTS TOMORROW (FULL WEEK CALENDAR)

*       US MACRO DATA TOMORROW: Challenger Job Cuts at 5:30am ET. Mortgage Applications at 7am ET. ADP Employment Change at 8:15am ET. PMI-Mfg (revision) at 9:45am ET. ISM-Mfg (New Orders/Employment/Prices Paid), Construction Spending at 10am ET. The required six-year formal joint review of the USMCA officially.

*       US EARNINGS TOMORROW: FDS, GIS

*       GLOBAL MACRO DATA TOMORROW: (UK) Nationwide House Price at 2am ET. (Eurozone) CPI/PPI at 5am ET. (Japan) Monetary Base YoY at 7:50pm ET. (Japan) Foreign Buying Japan Bonds at 7:50pm ET. (Japan) Foreign Buying Japan Stocks at 7:50pm ET.

 

JPM MARKET INTEL EQUITY & MACRO NARRATIVE

Market Intelligence will not be publishing July 2 вҖ“ July 6.

Stocks finished higher today led by Tech. Beyond the index level, price actions were not that supportive, with nearly 60% of SPX stocks actually finished lower. The most notable price actions was bond yield today: while upside surprises in JOLTS did fuel some hawkishness, the magnitude of the moves today suggested that the reversal of recent rebalancing momentum and anticipation of the upcoming NFP could contribute to these moves as well. Tomorrow, watch for ISM-Mfg, see the preview from Feroli below.

 

*       JPM ECON ON TODAYвҖҷS MACRO DATA (here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514903&ad=4537353171&h=_g8iGGk&sig=BTp9DWad8NYrrIlhXwksNyNpDHi&v=2&isig=HSEu2KObQvoSZ2xlLENck2jCOes&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-vu7jted/GPS-5353105-0/37539fe9-4bdf-465e-952c-42e15277b2a2> ) вҖ“ вҖңThe labor market message from the June consumer confidence survey and May JOLTS sent conflicting messages, with consumer confidence suggesting that unemployment may yet rise further while the JOLTS managed to hold onto the spike in job openings from the prior month. The consumer confidence report in particular is a reminder that amidst a notable improvement in a range of labor indicators, including payroll growth and jobless claims, there continues to be ongoing deterioration in some measures that have historically been correlated with the unemployment rate.вҖқ

*       PREVIEW FOR ISM-MFG (Feroli; here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514903&ad=4537353171&h=GfdmPbf&sig=IV91hG3BmbsJ8Ld0IGN7HJIodqX&v=2&isig=12y5ZDXLDrQ2OUjQlHyBKNoqSf4&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/nahe0bhi/GPS-5321939-0/37539fe9-4bdf-465e-952c-42e15277b2a2> ) вҖ“ вҖңWe expect the June ISM manufacturing index to tick up to 55.0 from 54.0 in the prior month, along with a broadly unchanged print of 56.0 on the final PMI manufacturing report. The ISM has been marking cycle-high levels since the large jump in January, with some of the recent improvement driven by front-loading behavior to get ahead of rising inflationary pressures and supply chain issues from the Middle East conflict, as noted in recent ISM and PMI press releases. Early signals from the flash PMI report and the regional manufacturing surveys point to continued strength in manufacturing. Since the spike in January, both the new orders and production metrics have been declining in the ISM report, before the improvement in May. The index for new orders moved to 56.8 in May from 57.1 in January, while the production index marked 54.3 in the latest report from 55.9 at the start of the year. This wedge between the two metrics has moved to the upper end of its recent range, which could point to a further rise in manufacturing activity in the upcoming print. The flash PMI manufacturing report saw moderating price pressures, likely reflecting receding global energy prices. However, the price index remains elevated.вҖқ

 

TRADING DESK COMMENTARY

*       BRIAN HEAVEY (TMT) вҖ“ As we mentioned yesterday, we saw notable demand across the hyperscalers (notable AMZN and GOOGL), which felt more of dip buying post rebalance (also the narrative being that these companies can manage their capex messaging, P/E's are at 10yr lows, and ultimately these co's stand to benefit as we move from frontier to optimization at the enterprise level = i.e. the AI Toll booth).

o   TELECOMs were in focus yesterday (they got hit hard) following 1) reports of CHTR discussing a mobile partnership with the large IPO that we cannot yet speak of 2) CMCSA breakup may eventually lead to a large merger in the broadband space (i.e. CHTR) 3) RKLB buying Iridiam stoking more fears of satellite competition.

o   Worth a flag --> VULNERABILITY MANAGEMENT - within security software. Brian Essex is adding TENB to the Focus List and Upgrading QLYS to Neutral as we believe this space stands to benefit with the uptick in use cases for cheaper, open sourced Chinese AI models.

 

US MKT INTELвҖҷS NFP SCENARIO ANALYSIS

FeroliвҖҷs full NFP preview is here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514903&ad=4537353171&h=GfdmPbf&sig=IV91hG3BmbsJ8Ld0IGN7HJIodqX&v=2&isig=12y5ZDXLDrQ2OUjQlHyBKNoqSf4&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/nahe0bhi/GPS-5321939-0/37539fe9-4bdf-465e-952c-42e15277b2a2> . He sees 125k jobs being added, versus the StreetвҖҷs estimate of 110k and 172k jobs in the prior print. For the unemployment rate (U.3) he sees 4.3% vs. 4.3% prior. For Average Hourly Earnings, he sees +0.2% MoM and +3.4% YoY.

 

The following scenario analysis is NOT A PRODUCT OF JPM RESEARCH, this is a trading desk view from JPM US Market Intelligence. 

 

*       [5%] NFP prints above 160k. SPX loses 50bp to gains 1.5%

*       [25%] NFP prints between 130k вҖ“160k. SPX gains 75bp вҖ“ 1.25%

*       [40%] NFP prints between 100k вҖ“ 130k. SSPX gains 50bp вҖ“ 1%

*       [25%] NFP prints between 70k вҖ“ 100k. SPX loses 50bp вҖ“ 1.25%

*       [5%] NFP prints below 70k. SPX loses 1.25% вҖ“ 2%

*       WHAT ARE OPTIONS PRICING? For options expiring on July 2, 2026, using data from June 29, show a 1.2% implied move.

*       US MKT INTEL ON NFP вҖ“ With period-end rebalancing appearing to be completed, we look for the bullish trend to resume. Dip-buying behavior remains prevalent, which is why the outcomes here skew bullishly, especially when considering neutral aggregate positioning. When considering macro fundamentals, FeroliвҖҷs estimate reflects a resilient economy, and our latest Consumer Cash Pile (here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514903&ad=4537353171&h=RdT9eEp&sig=7w1j9qPFjfFkZl9kdWKAEz0z0lO&v=2&isig=U6Q0cLXUqGCxQ9KvLGp5uR1DmjS&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/t1b49b5u/GPS-5351659-0.pdf/37539fe9-4bdf-465e-952c-42e15277b2a2> ) reflects ample levels of cash across the income brackets that drive the majority of US consumption with minimal signs of aggregate consumer stress. Combine that with a likely robust 26Q2 earnings season and that should drive stocks higher, ceteris paribus. For 26Q2 earnings season, FactSet expectations are for revenue growth of 12.1% (vs. 11.9% in 26Q1), earnings growth of 22.0% (vs. 28.8% in 26Q1), with 14.1% margins (vs. 14.8% in 26Q1, which was the second consecutive record).

 



Source: JP Morgan Economics

 

US MKT INTEL VIEWS  вҖ“ below are published in the June 29th Morning Briefing

We maintain our Tactical Bullish call despite the potential for some additional choppiness into month-end from both period-end rebalancing and the US / Iran conflict. We look for markets to stabilize and trade higher into earnings which unofficially kick off on July 14. A few thoughts followed by the Monetization Menu and the balance of the note.

*       NEW MONETIZATION MENU вҖ“ We think it makes sense to maintain the Tech / Cyclicals barbell while adding Healthcare exposure as low correlated long. Within Tech, we like AI, Memory, and Semis despite the recent pullback and elevated vol regime, so we would consider adding some hedges from other parts of Tech or factor hedges such as Beta / Momentum. Regarding Cyclicals, a combination of Airlines, Banks, Homebuilders, Retailers, and Transports; we would avoid Autos given USMCA risk. In Healthcare, we like broad-based exposure across the sector including Biotech, MedTech, and Pharma. Internationally, we Korea, parts of Latam, and Taiwan and would consider hedging with Indian Equities. For book hedges, consider SPX puts with July expiry.

*       DELTA-ONE TRADE IDEAS (Manish Sinha, June 26) вҖ“ (i) Short Beta (JPBPURE Index) and Momentum (JPMPURE Index) factors to hedge alpha dispersion risk and to play tightening financial conditions. (ii) Short Residual Vol aka ResVol (JPRVPURE Index) to hedge a hawkish Fed and worsening liquidity; (iii) Short Low Profitability Crowding (JPLPCRW2 Index) as a crowding hedge for AI-heavy exposure; (iv) +Long Dispersion Laggards (JPDISPL Index) vs. -Short Dispersion Winners (JPDISPW Index) to play a dispersion reversal.

*       OLD MONETIZATION MENU (JUNE 23) вҖ“ Unchanged WoW, we would like to return to the Tech and Cyclical barbell but stay nimble on the rate-sensitive or Hormuz reopening trades: stay long in the global AI theme with heavier weight on US vs. Asia. We do think the initial вҖңeverything rallyвҖқ should benefit Airlines, Housing and Retail but historical patterns reflect that the rally could be short-lived and more vulnerable to the shifting headline volatility. We like a tactical long in Financials into the July earnings season and expect it to catch up with the broader Cyclicals, as strong fundamental growth remains intact.

 

NEWS LINKS

emails.body:
North America Equity Research 	

US Auto Retail Ecosystem


5-min Read: Top #10 Themes From Recent Corporate Access, Including June Conference Call Series, Dealer Tours (SoCal and UK), NIADA Convention, Pre-Quiet Period Checks, and more...

 	 
By Rajat Gupta AC <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=-XvkyAe&sig=QxC91pn1QlT9DZ4pEFHKiFzxu8N&v=2&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/analyst/R252165> , Jash Patwa <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=vrcqvTU&sig=18GCIuZ13ErQNdKsLQKafj9zwe3&v=2&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/analyst/I749540> , Yash Beswala <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=2u-fuwU&sig=FCvT8HWScMEpJPdtObleIMBdAdq&v=2&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/analyst/F707573>  

Click here for the full document and disclaimers <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=btr7M1d&sig=HuwLkkXMV9fesyk9mQpITgWhDjY&v=2&isig=YrFSw0xibzOvJ5NbkDpT1D6S7as&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-21leteq/GPS-5345376-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> 

Our 7th Annual Auto Retail Ecosystem June Conference Call Series wrapped up yesterday, featuring seven engaging fireside chats with public franchise dealers (ABG <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=Mq5jKuJ&sig=NdCpEbIwnYxKo3y3ok222w2Lr3d&v=2&isig=3j6NlG6x3s02VewG1QoxoMigRaJ&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/gg7od958/GPS-5332845-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , GPI <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=kN40mrb&sig=7Z7807IsYUozl6xZ8Z5O9WjdTNg&v=2&isig=Mu9IdzJVaq305L0ij87F8hAhccS&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-qmld0nn/GPS-5329481-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , LAD <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=tEwjCG-&sig=4A5EGrVY7aUDD20Bav4EHexWttE&v=2&isig=IzDmTequm0VxUHel9U79VNU3UFL&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/j08roi99/GPS-5351809-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , PAG <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=-CuS1jd&sig=WT6Qjjqo9OQOOSu7BiS9JVkhxf7&v=2&isig=8FdJK81GOboIQ7oTS50uLMNcy9A&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-hgnj9pn/GPS-5334118-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , and SAH <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=6wDjccj&sig=S4ozOzgOqO9ucNSWmVLP2jphfSW&v=2&isig=OSxfImEEOjVFqaT9andberavcvu&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/l111uh33/GPS-5341675-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> ) and industry experts (The Presidio Group <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=rAL2oDm&sig=1MiX3poxWTcXTyaGGTJGNNZAQf0&v=2&isig=9pz6YS4I6hgiEYuFIVccMl1s1VU&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/k1clkmiv/GPS-5337614-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20>  and NAAA <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=HGdHe6A&sig=3kutddReS5kyVsYrMpa6xctgkA8&v=2&isig=CvNT13dEuCG7haCAatmuxreV3pc&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/kk45o5s6/GPS-5345807-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> ). We gained insights into near-term industry trends, expectations for 2H26, and explored key themes relevant to the auto retail ecosystem. We also recently attended the NIADA (National Independent Automobile Dealers Association) Convention in Denver, CO, and our key takeaways are folded in below. See our recap notes on ABG <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=Mq5jKuJ&sig=NdCpEbIwnYxKo3y3ok222w2Lr3d&v=2&isig=3j6NlG6x3s02VewG1QoxoMigRaJ&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/gg7od958/GPS-5332845-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , GPI <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=kN40mrb&sig=7Z7807IsYUozl6xZ8Z5O9WjdTNg&v=2&isig=Mu9IdzJVaq305L0ij87F8hAhccS&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-qmld0nn/GPS-5329481-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , LAD <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=fFhZi--&sig=EiHGgLnTBdeNESMw62QLqb9A5ww&v=2&isig=P1ur77njn23SRF0GduatIq3tXV9&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/ogjpemci/GPS-5351434-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , PAG <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=-CuS1jd&sig=WT6Qjjqo9OQOOSu7BiS9JVkhxf7&v=2&isig=8FdJK81GOboIQ7oTS50uLMNcy9A&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-hgnj9pn/GPS-5334118-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> , SAH <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=yDgnnkR&sig=9jcQLRQRQenW4CN6kduN8eI8KAE&v=2&isig=2yfQbA713NVZIIMTYzFt6rhN4fy&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-f4r5t3b/GPS-5341660-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20>  and The Presidio Group <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=r_6oWbP&sig=Bg3Z9teiCIePjHdHQLCJGGuZZLg&v=2&isig=YclVfZVZFcbzMJMCj0VkynXnlpK&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/mk5n5k4k/GPS-5337261-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> . We also hosted 5 publicly traded dealers for a tour in SoCal last month (click here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=XWSJS9o&sig=9NzRcMIenyu4Exyw65mLLyfQ4Bi&v=2&isig=HfhzF73kbgEo3OieMNhDaFe2GRj&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-rku4286/GPS-5318498-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> ), visited Chinese brand dealerships of LAD and PAG in UK (click here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=rQ-mKBp&sig=VPaATgdz3K4JbM5HzQBWdHg81Lb&v=2&isig=ZnWqbRKNRerru4GaRjhhtXrzR3r&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/15t7omil/GPS-5332730-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> ), and recently published our 7th annual dealer survey (click here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=3rjxxSz&sig=Omrnvk0u1raUk34vMlhRWbSNrI5&v=2&isig=M4PIr1Z01yAwSDW9boddKgZKl5M&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/tlami3a8/GPS-5337491-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> ). Below we highlight our top 10 themes: 

*	Consumers continue to hold on. Management teams broadly described a steady but cautious consumer, with no clear deterioration in demand yet but affordability intensifying as the gating factor. June SAAR trends reinforce this with industry-wide expectations tracking in the ~16.1-16.5 mn range (JPMe 16.2 mn, click here <https://jpmorgan.email.streetcontxt.net/platform/al?a=13514577&ad=4537098845&h=qVwn0f_&sig=PeNWETDXD0fYSYnVUGRCPEC6yLY&v=2&isig=4Qs5chkz3AzXTUic0sEJufuJlJP&url=https://markets.jpmorgan.com/research/email/scx/-sh7akp3/GPS-5343838-0/97d3a26f-0dc5-41a4-b15f-968b163a3f20> ). Consumer strain is on display in stretched loan terms, payment pressure, and elevated used vehicle pricing rather than outright volume loss. LAD flagged negative equity trending higher and getting back to historical levels. Brands and dealer groups that over-index to luxury dealers are positioned better than the broader market as higher income demand holds up. Notably, 3P vendors and independent dealers at the NIADA Convention flagged immigration enforcement as driving softening in used car markets in Texas and Florida, also contributing to incremental auto credit losses. 
*	Gas prices and the Middle East conflict remain overhangs. The gas price spike tied to the Middle East conflict was the most consistently cited near-term concern, viewed as weighing on confidence across both new vehicles and service. The prevailing characterization is deferral rather than mix shift, with little evidence yet of buyers trading large SUVs for smaller, more fuel-efficient vehicles. The earliest signs of strain are concentrated in truck-heavy markets such as California and Texas, where higher pump prices are pushing some purchases out, though the softness is generally viewed as temporary and confidence driven rather than structural. 
*	New GPU stable to modestly lower, but tariff pass-through still ahead. Consensus points to stable to gradual new vehicle GPU normalization in 2Q rather than a sharp reset, with the back half trajectory hinging on tariffs. SAH was a touch cautious, expecting GPUs to absorb the bulk of affordability and tariff strain, whether OEMs pass cost on to consumers or to dealers (model year changeover later this year is the most likely pass-through window), and ABG similarly sees GPUs grinding toward the ~$3K range (vs. ~$3.3K in 1Q), requiring modest incremental discounting. GPI and LAD suggested GPUs have stabilized, citing several consecutive quarters of stable new vehicle GPUs, with LAD tying stability to the supply/demand equilibrium and a steady SAAR, while GPI noted no meaningful tariff burden into 2027 model year pricing observed yet in initial automaker conversations. JLR stop-sale in late June and early July could have partial implications for near-term new GPU вҖ” SAH/PAG have the most exposure in coverage. 
*	Used GPUs holding firm on tight supply. Used GPU is proving resilient, with constrained supply the primary support and trade-in dependency keeping used volume tethered to new. On potential GPU risks from growing off-lease supply, PAG sees lease returns as a measured trickle against still tight inventory, while GPI cited sustained inventory discipline (~30 days supply) as a buffer. ABG pivoted in mid-May toward used volume at the expense of GPU to drive throughput, executed selectively at stores with the recon capacity and operator quality to support it, with the trade-off more visible into 2H. LAD is also turning to GPU recapture, and SAH expects used GPUs within its guidance range and downplayed competitive pressure from KMX, citing a fragmented market where direct overlap rarely materializes. A consistent theme at the NIADA Convention was the ongoing consolidation in the independent used dealer industry (tempered attendance at the convention vs. previous years was itself indicative of this dynamic), especially as subprime consumer credit remains stressed amidst affordability headwinds. 
*	Off-lease EV supply reframed as an opportunity. The group was uniformly constructive on the incoming wave of off-lease EVs, treating it as a sourcing opportunity rather than a residual value threat, further reinforced by NAAA commentary citing strong auction buyer appetite for said vehicles. ABG expects OEMs to lean on CPO subvention and used lease programs to keep returns out of third-party auctions, allowing it to source attractively while protecting residuals, and is confident the supply can be absorbed without material GPU impact. GPI and PAG framed right of first refusal and scaled footprints as competitive advantages, declining underwater units back to the captive while channeling attractive vehicles through CPO, and SAH sees EchoPark as a natural outlet for any supply overflow. A common kicker, flagged by both ABG and GPI, is that higher gas prices are lifting near-term demand and pricing for used EVs, with the larger residual hits seen as already behind the industry. Our conversations with industry participants at the NIADA Convention suggested independent dealers stepping in to source EVs viewed as affordable buys for consumers, though the industry remains undereducated on EV reconditioning/appraisals, leaving scaled players (like CVNA and KMX as well as public dealers) better suited to remarket these vehicles. 
*	P&S growth viewed as durable, though tough warranty comps finally hitting and affordability concerns creeping in. P&S remains a steady source of growth, with a MSD% y/y growth expectation the consensus medium-term guidepost across dealer groups, underpinned by capacity expansion, retention and market share initiatives. Near-term variability is mostly company-specific вҖ” ABG expects LSD% y/y growth in 2Q/3Q on software conversion headwinds before reverting to its medium-term MSD% pace, while PAG and LAD both flagged tougher warranty comps against elevated prior year recall activity. GPI called out a trough in the 4-7 year old cohort tied to the pandemic-era low in new sales, pressuring repair order counts in the heart of the service sweet spot, to which the company is responding via data-driven reactivation of that cohort, competitive oil pricing, and stepped up aftersales marketing. We are also sensing from managementвҖҷs tone that affordability concerns are starting to modestly creep into the traditional consumer base after multiple years of labor rates/parts pricing inflation. 
*	UK on a better trajectory albeit still challenging. GPI, PAG and LAD see the UK backdrop as improving but still challenging. GPI pointed to sustained service momentum, while used demand has softened into May after a strong March, and is working through portfolio rationalization, having disposed half of the JLR stores with incremental divestitures expected over the coming months. LAD is porting its US playbook of deeper used penetration, F&I attachment and aftersales growth. PAGвҖҷs heavily premium UK book is holding up better than the broader market, though it flagged the 33% ZEV mandate running well ahead of actual demand mix that is tracking closer to ~23%. On agency model, Mercedes and MINI store economics are tracking in line with expectations and the coming BMW transition to agency is a key watch item, while other brands have largely retreated from agency. 
*	AI as a margin and productivity lever. AI is viewed as an avenue to expand margins and capture more of the gross profit pool вҖ” ABG has automated call answering and appointment setting at scale, enabling call center consolidation, and is applying AI to service lane cycle time tracking and back office accounting. GPI pointed to a broad slate of AI and automation projects across back office, call center, marketing, and technician productivity, while SAH is pursuing both internal cost out and customer facing enhancements, including positioning inventory for AI driven search as that channel develops. Presidio, citing conversations with private franchise dealers, noted that adoption is still early and weighed against limited patience for unproven returns. 
*	Capital allocation: M&A pipeline improving. Capital allocation is pivoting toward acquisitions across the group as the deal environment opens up. SAH placed M&A at the top of its priority list, skewing to franchise and low capital powersports deals, while GPI noted evaluating deals weekly with a preference for cluster geographies, balanced against a commitment to de-leverage. AN is likely to see a drop in buyback run-rate following 2 sizable acquisitions in June. PAG reiterated comfort flexing leverage for the right opportunity. ABG signaled a near-term lever for buybacks as management is comfortable pushing de-leveraging trajectory a couple quarters out, citing attractive valuation. LAD was more balanced, tilting toward buybacks while describing the M&A pipeline as incrementally better but still populated by buyers willing to overpay. The backdrop is supportive as well, with Presidio noting that industry deal activity is running at record levels, with public groups remaining active buyers. 
*	Chinese OEMs: a UK opportunity now, a US overhang later. LAD and PAG have scaled furthest, leveraging vacant sites to keep entry capex low, and PAG noting GPUs in line with other UK brands and partners disciplined on over-dealering, while GPI is taking a more measured approach, onboarding Geely and in discussions with additional brands but cautioning that F&I and service monetization remain underdeveloped given a limited installed base. GPI also noted Chinese brands gaining ICE as well as EV traction, taking share largely from mainstream volume brands. In the US, Presidio sees the Chinese brandsвҖҷ entry as a credible long-term overhang, with domestic full-size pickups the most defensible segment and lower-priced import brands the most exposed вҖ” LAD is in active dialogue to become a partner for Chinese brands in Canada and would look to partner further if they were allowed to expand into the US.

Capstone, 

The next tech IPO wave is already taking shape, and the biggest signals are showing up before the filings. 

Our recent reporting revealed that Kalshi has passed $2 billion in annualized revenue and held informal IPO talks, while OpenAIвҖҷs $3.7 billion first-quarter cash burn raises new questions about how investors will judge AI companies heading toward the public markets. Space companies including Sierra Space, Vast Space and Axiom Space are also plotting listings as SpaceX fuels IPO momentum. 

Behind the headlines is a broader question investors, founders and operators are watching closely: Which major tech company will go public next? More importantly, when and how? 

Our IPO Tracker <https://e.customeriomail.>  follows the most notable private tech companies moving toward the public markets, tracking their plans, progress and outlook amid changing market conditions.

emails.subject: PULSE: Circle Internet Group Inc. (CRCL.N): Thoughts on Open Standard Announcement, Coinbase Partnership, and Composition of Stablecoin Activity
emails.body:
Circle Internet Group Inc. (CRCL.N) 	
Thoughts on Open Standard Announcement, Coinbase Partnership, and Composition of Stablecoin Activity	 
Read Full Report <https://ny.matrix.ms.com/eqr/article/webapp/4b5a59a0-748a-11f1-9560-223b52d1e427?t=1782846664:1708:17494:acba1c0882e9&m=1&ch=CDF Research ISG#e=ZmVsaXBlLm1vbnRlaXJvQGNhcHN0b25lLmNvbS5icg==> 	 
(PDF: 10 Pages) 	
North America 	
Stock Rating   Equal-weight
Price Target   $106.00

James E Faucette, Michael N Infante, Meryl R Thomas, CFA, Shefali Tamaskar	 
June 30, 2026

	

What happened? Earlier this morning, Open Standard announced Open USD, a new US dollar-backed stablecoin for global money movement, backed by a broad consortium of more than 100 fintechs, payment networks, banks, crypto firms, and technology platforms, including Visa, Stripe, BNY, BlackRock, Klarna, Chime, Alphabet, Coinbase, Mastercard, Shopify, DoorDash, Corpay, and others. The stablecoin is expected to go live later this year, with partners planning to integrate it into their systems. The stated goal is to build a stablecoin that is open, low-cost, high-volume, broadly accessible, and better aligned with business users by enabling free minting and redemption, shared reserve economics, and collaborative governance through an independent Open Standard entity.

Open Standard looks like a competitive risk for Circle from both a governance perspective...Open USD appears predicated on collaborative governance, with Open Standard operated by an independent company whose board is made up of Open USD partners. That contrasts with USDCвҖҷs more Circle-centric model, where Circle controls USDC governance. We find DoorDashвҖҷs (covered by Brian Nowak) commentsвҖ”"in building payout products for Dashers and merchants in the U.S., weвҖҷve seen firsthand how meaningful faster, more affordable access to earnings can be. Stablecoins give us a real path to bring that level of financial flexibility to other geographies we operate in. What sets Open USD apart is that itвҖҷs genuinely open: no single company controls it, and the partners building on it have a seat at the table. ThatвҖҷs the right foundation for moving money for everyone"вҖ”particularly interesting, as it implies collaborate governance is a key reason Open USD will be attractive to large distribution platforms. As a reminder, DASH recently announced a partnership with CRCL where it would leverage USDC for international driver payouts.

...as well as the underlying revenue model. Open Standard says partners receive the earnings generated on Open USD reserves, less a small management fee for operating costs, creating a broader default revenue-sharing model for consortium participants. Based on disclosed arrangements, CircleвҖҷs model appears more negotiated and distribution-led, with economics shared with partners such as Coinbase and Binance based on platform balances and treasury holdings. As a result, we think the key distinction is less that Circle cannot share yield and more that Open Standard is attempting to embed shared economics and shared governance into the core design of the stablecoin from inception.

CoinbaseвҖҷs inclusion in the Open Standard consortium may not complicate the August renewal, but could be increasing the temperature. We still think it is very unlikely Coinbase voluntarily steps away from the Circle partnership because the deal remains quite favorable. Coinbase can continue building Coinbase Payments, push more commerce and settlement activity onto Base, and participate in Open Standard in pursuit of strategic optionality, all while preserving its USDC economics. As a reminder, Coinbase keeps 100% of the yield generated by USDC held on its own platform and receives 50% of the residual economics from off-platform USDC activity after contractual deductions. From a contractual perspective, the agreement was last was renewed on came on August 18, 2023, with the initial three-year term expiring in August 2026. From our read of the threshold criteria, we believe it's highly unlikely either party doesn't satisfy its obligations and thus, while Open Standard and CircleвҖҷs Arc both raise the strategic tension, Coinbase remains in a strong position with respect to its ability to monetize USDC, build Base, expand payments, and preserve strategic optionality at the same time.

USDC activity is still predominantly crypto trading in nature with BTC weakness resulting in stablecoin market cap compression. Per Exhibit 1,stablecoin activity remains overwhelmingly skewed to crypto trading and transfer activity vs. payments. McKinsey estimates roughly $80T of annualized stablecoin volume, of which $45T reflects technical transfers and roughly $35T reflects trading and other nonpayment financial activity, leaving only $0.39T of payments, or roughly 0.5% of unadjusted activity and about 1% of adjusted activity. That aligns with what we've observed as well, in that use cases beyond consumer remittances and in-country spending on USD-backed stablecoin-linked cards, remains limited. Additionally, while YTD changes in USDC market cap (down ~2% YTD) have fared meaningfully better vs. BTC (down ~32%), we continue to believe that meaningful expansion of USDC in market cap (where the company believes USDC market cap will continue to CAGR at ~40%) is predicated on real-world use cases that appear nascent and unlikely to meaningful inflect over the near-term (Exhibit 2, Exhibit 3).

The majority of stablecoin activity is still for non-payments use cases (e.g., crypto trading 
 
Source: McKinsey & Company 
USDC Market Cap vs. Bitcoin Price 
 
Source: CoinMarketCap, Morgan Stanley Research

emails.subject: INVITEвҖ”Sen. Kristen Gonzalez (D-NY) on NYвҖҷs Data Center Pause & U.S. AI Debate
emails.body:
<https://www.jefferies.com>   	 
June 30, 2026	 
	Global | Washington Strategy	 
 <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/links2/html/222a8ff7-ece3-4dc7-b4a6-14ce1f374d77> 	 	 
INVITEвҖ”Sen. Kristen Gonzalez (D-NY) on NYвҖҷs Data Center Pause & U.S. AI Debate <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/links2/html/222a8ff7-ece3-4dc7-b4a6-14ce1f374d77> 	 
New York has moved to the forefront of the national debate with a potential one-year pause on data center construction by Sen. Gonzalez (see here <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/docs/html/8a9f616f-c0d7-42bc-9a06-35ce65de2f96.html>  & here <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/docs/html/80ad0728-aa82-4316-bfb0-29dcec358f88.html> ). On this call, we will discuss the billвҖҷs key provisions and what New YorkвҖҷs proposal signals for the broader national conversation on AI infrastructure. Join us with Sen. Kristen Gonzalez, Chair of the New York State Senate Internet and Technology Committee, on Mon, July 20 @ 11AM ET. REGISTER <https://jefferies-sustainability-transition-strategy.open-exchange.net/> 


Background


New York has moved to the forefront of the national debate with the Responsible Data Center Development Act, omnibus legislation sponsored by Sen. Kristen Gonzalez (see here <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/docs/html/8a9f616f-c0d7-42bc-9a06-35ce65de2f96.html>  & here <https://javatar.bluematrix.com/docs/html/80ad0728-aa82-4316-bfb0-29dcec358f88.html> ). The bill would place a one-year pause on permits for new large data centers while the state evaluates their community impacts, and is now awaiting a decision from the governor.

On this call, we will discuss the billвҖҷs key provisions and what New YorkвҖҷs proposal signals for the broader national conversation on AI infrastructure. Join us with Sen. Kristen Gonzalez, Chair of the New York State Senate Internet and Technology Committee.


Date & Registration


Monday, July 20, at 11:00 AM ET

https://jefferies-sustainability-transition-strategy.open-exchange.net/ <https://urldefense.com/v3/__https:/jefferies-sustainability-transition-strategy.open-exchange.net/__;!!PePb2utDNNs!vpjyBYHgJkiab63ytE5URvaT0dsBa694Nx818QE4TSFcFtdVzkM2fU1eHKutV7jKn39Wy5XYEjiNJHgjcqge7wzvX4LIeV8$> 


Jefferies Hosts


*	Aniket Shah, Global Head of Washington, Sustainability & Transition Strategy
*	Yujin Kim, Washington, Sustainability & Transition Strategy Senior Associate


Jefferies Speaker


Senator Kristen Gonzalez represents New York StateвҖҷs 59th District and serves as Chair of the Senate Internet and Technology Committee and the Elections Committee. Her district includes parts of Western Queens, Northern Brooklyn, and the East Side of Manhattan. Prior to her election, she worked in the New York City Council, the Obama White House, and in Senator Chuck SchumerвҖҷs office. She also has experience in the technology sector. In the State Senate, her legislative work has included issues related to data privacy, artificial intelligence, and election integrity. Senator Gonzalez graduated from Columbia University and has been involved in local initiatives, including the Western Queens Community Land Trust, service on her community board, and a citywide campaign for public internet.
